If he checks back I’ll see the river and reevaluate only if I pair or the river card and action create a credible bluffing opportunity.

On a board of 3h-Js-3c-3s, the texture has become highly specific. I have three-of-a-kind with a Queen kicker. However, Minni called my flop continuation bet, which strongly suggests she has a Jack, a pocket pair, or a strong Ace. Since the board now has three 3s, any Jack or pocket pair currently beats me (forming a full house). My Queen-high has very little showdown value if the hand is checked down, but Minni's tendency to be 'sticky' (calling the flop) makes a second bluff on the turn risky. By checking back, I take a free card and avoid being check-raised, while keeping the pot manageable in case I happen to be ahead of a draw or a lower high-card hand.

Pot is 1,250 and it’s checked to me on the river (board 3h Js 3c 3s As). I hold Q♦T♠ — no pair, no meaningful showdown value. Villain opened preflop and c‑bet a small 300 on the flop which I called; both of us checked the turn when the third 3 came. That turn massively polarizes his range toward strong holdings (Jx → full houses, any 3 in hand → quads, many pocket pairs turn into very strong hands) while also containing some missed c‑bet bluffs (AK, KQ, etc.). The river A helps a portion of his checking range (Ax) and doesn’t meaningfully increase my fold equity — Q/T don’t block A or J. To profitably bluff here I’d need a large enough fold frequency (e.g. >~33% on a half‑pot bet) which is unrealistic against his line. There’s no real value to betting either. Best play is pot control: check and give up to a river bet. If he checks back we go to showdown; if he bets I fold.

On a board of 3h-Js-3c-3s-As, there are three 3s on the board. My best five-card hand is 3-3-3-A-Q. Minni called my flop bet and has checked through the turn and river. If Minni has any pair (an Ace, a Jack, or any pocket pair), she has a full house and beats me. If she has no pair, her best hand is 3-3-3-A and the highest of her other cards or the Jack on the board. Since I have a Queen, I beat hands like K-high (if her other card is lower than a Jack) or any other high-card hand lower than a Queen. There is no value in betting here, as better hands (any full house) will not fold and worse hands will not call. Checking back allows for a free showdown.

Short stack of 1000 (~10BB) on the button with K8 suited, a strong speculative hand with good playability, position, and suited broadway potential. Facing a small to-call of 100 into a 150 pot (pot odds ~2.5:1 if called), likely a limp-raise or min-raise from one blind (precise action unclear but small sizing). Opponents are very deep (Minni 24k+, Flash 14k+). Optimal short-stack strategy is to raise aggressively for fold equity and build pot with strong hands. History shows opponents fold decently to aggression preflop/postflop. Raising to 400 (4x the facing bet, ~40% stack) pressures blinds, gets value from worse kings/connectors, and sets up for shove if reraised. Excellent fold equity given depths and sizing. Postflop position maintained if called.

Facing a button open to 400 from the BTN with the SB posted (pot 550, I must call 350). I hold J♦7♠ — an off‑suit, marginal hand with poor postflop playability and a weak kicker that will be dominated by a large portion of the button’s opening range. Calling 350 into a 550 pot (pot becomes 900) requires ~39% equity to justify a call; versus a BTN open (and with the BB still to act) my true equity is well below that in multiway/ OOP situations. A bluff 3‑bet to 700 is thin with this hand — I’d be risking a lot to isolate or face a shove/call from better holdings. Prefer to preserve a big stack and wait for a better spot, so I fold preflop.

I am in the Big Blind with K5o. Elon, on the Button, has raised to 400. However, I notice Elon only has 600 chips remaining in his stack total (based on the provided state 'Elon (Button): 600'). If he raised to 400, he only has 200 chips behind. This makes it an effective all-in situation for his very short stack. K5o is a decent hand to defend against a button shove from such a short stack, especially since I only need to call 300 more to win a total pot that will likely be his entire stack plus blinds. However, given the context of a Cash Game where I want to maximize my stack and avoid unnecessary variance against extremely short stacks if I don't have to, but mathematically K5o is ahead of a wide button shoving range. Actually, looking at the chip counts, Elon has already committed 400 of his 600. I will call to see a flop, as I have the odds to defend against a player with only 2BBs left behind.

I am out of position against Elon, who has a very short stack of 600 chips remaining. The board is 4s-8s-7d, and I have King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6). Given that Elon was the pre-flop aggressor and has less than a pot-sized bet remaining, I will check to him. If he shoves, I will have to evaluate my equity (overcards plus the gutshot) against his likely range to see if it's worth calling the remaining 600, but for now, checking is the standard play.

Short stack of 600 (~6BB effective) on button with top pair top kicker (88 with K) on a drawy flop (4s8s7d: spade flush draw, open-enders like 56/59/9T). Flash checked BB after calling preflop raise, showing possible weakness. Pot 850, shoving 600 (~70% pot) maximizes fold equity against draws/bluffs while extracting value from worse pairs/draws/overcards. Checking risks giving free card to spades/straights or induces check-back but forgoes value given depths (Flash 14k). History shows Flash calls but folds to aggression often (e.g., Hand 49, 6). Optimal short-stack play: jam for thin value + protection.

In this hand, Elon is effectively all-in for his remaining stack. I am holding King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6) on a 4-8-7 board. The pot is currently 1450, and I need to call 600 more to see the remaining cards. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3.4 to 1. Given that Elon's range for a short-stack shove includes many hands I might be ahead of or have significant equity against (like smaller overcards or air), and the fact that I have a King as an overcard and a gutshot, the mathematical price is right to call and realize my equity.

I’m on the button with 9♠6♦ — a marginal hand but strong positional advantage. Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call; the table’s standard open size has been ~300, so I’ll make a standard button steal attempt. Raising to 300 gives me fold equity against the blinds and lets me play the flop in position if Flash (SB) calls. Elon (BB) is short (~1,950, ~19.5bb) and may shove over the raise; I won’t call a short-stack shove with 9-6o (or a large 3-bet) — I’ll fold to significant aggression. Overall a 300 open maximizes long-term EV here versus passively limping/calling.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s 5c, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised to 300 from the button. Even though I'm getting a slight discount to call from the SB, this hand plays poorly post-flop and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the strength of my hand and the position, folding is the standard and most profitable play.

J♣3♥ is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing a button raise from Minni, who is aggressive and has a massive stack, my hand has negligible equity against her likely wide but still superior range. I'm OOP post-flop, pot odds are insufficient (200 to call into 450, ~31% implied but poor playability), and with a 19.5BB effective stack, folding preserves chips for better spots like premium hands where I can leverage position or initiative.

I am on the button with 6d4d, a suited one-gapper. The stacks are quite shallow for Elon (19 BBs) and I have a significant stack. While 64s is a speculative hand, in a 3-handed game or small table, opening from the button is generally profitable to put pressure on the blinds. However, given the specific stack sizes, Elon's short stack makes him more likely to jam or play aggressively. But 100 to call (or raising) is standard. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop.

Preflop from the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x). My hand is K7o, offsuit with a weak kicker, not strong enough to defend against a wide button opening range. Pot is 450, calling 250 commits 13% of my 1900 stack, and I have poor postflop playability. Minni still to act in BB and could squeeze. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

Facing a button open to 300 with 2c9c in the big blind. Pot is 450; to call 200 (≈30.8% pot odds). 9-2 suited is a very weak holding — low realized equity and poor post‑flop playability out of position. A min 3-bet to 500 would be a weak bluff with little fold equity; calling puts me OOP in many multi-street pots where I’ll be dominated or only have backdoor chances. With deep stacks I want to pick better spots to invest chips, so I fold and preserve my stack for stronger opportunities.

3-handed on the button with A6s, a strong suited ace hand with good playability and position. Standard open-raise from this spot pre-flop. Players behind are deep-stacked (Minni 244xBB, Flash 135xBB), my stack is 19BB, so a standard 3x raise to 300 builds the pot while maintaining fold equity and allowing post-flop flexibility. Limping is suboptimal on the button; shoving is too aggressive for A6s. History shows opponents can be aggressive but often fold to button raises.

I have J♥T♦ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (to call 250 more). JTo is a playable hand with good flop playability against a BTN opening range. I prefer to call (total 300) rather than make a thin min‑raise to 500: a small raise keeps me out of position and will often invite the big blind to step in or induce a 3‑bet. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity postflop, and preserves options if the short‑stack button jams (I’ll re‑evaluate with pot odds if he shoves).

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 7h. Elon raised from the button to 300, and Minni called from the small blind. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 to call, giving me pot odds of 4.5:1. Elon is on a very short stack (1,600 total), which increases the likelihood that he is playing a wider range to double up. Given the excellent price and the fact that I am closing the action, I will call to see a flop.

Flop: Qd Kh 5s; my hand Jh Td — no pair but I have the straight draw to either an A or a 9 (8 outs). That’s roughly ~31–32% to hit by the river. I’m out of position in a three‑way pot (Elon opened, Flash cold‑called). Against two opponents my fold equity with a c‑bet is low and I don’t block K/Q/A combos, so a continuation bet will often be called by Kx/Qx/Ax or by floats from the big blind. 

Pot math: current pot is 900. With ~32% equity, calling a single bet B is break‑even when B ≈ 423 (B/(900+B) = 0.32). That means I should be willing to call small bets (roughly ≤ ~420) to realize this draw but not overcommit to larger bets without improving. 

Plan: Check and see how the two opponents react. If checked around I get a free turn to improve; if faced with a small bet I can call for proper pot odds and implied odds (and re‑evaluate on the turn); if faced with a large bet or a shove (especially from the short‑stack button) I’ll fold since my current hand has poor showdown value and insufficient equity to call big sizing. 

Action: check.

I have 8-high on a board of Qd-Kh-5s with no draws and no pair. The pre-flop raiser, Elon, is a short stack and still to act behind me. Minni has already checked. There is no reason to bluff into this board given my lack of equity and the stack sizes. I will check and see what the pre-flop aggressor does.

Post-flop on the button with A6s after raising pre-flop. Pot is 900, my stack 1600 (16BB effective, short). Board Qd Kh 5s is somewhat coordinated (broadway potential, spade draw possible) but rainbow-ish with three suits. Checked to me, standard spot to c-bet as pre-flop raiser to represent strength from button range, which includes many Kx, Qx, Ax pairs and draws. A♦ blocks AK/AQ, providing fold equity against floats or weak pairs. Opponents deep but history shows they fold to aggression (e.g., multiple folds to post-flop bets). Half-pot bet (450) too small for fold equity; sizing to 600 (2/3 pot) builds pot while leaving room for turns/rivers if called, and with short stack, denies equity effectively. Checking risks giving free cards to draws and loses value from my perceived strong range.

Preflop I elected to limp/call with J♦T♥ for flop playability. On this Q♦ K♥ 5♠ flop Elon bets 600 into the ~900 pot (pot now ~1500) and Flash checks behind. To call I must put in 600 to win ~1500, so the break-even equity is 600/(900+600) = 40%. My hand (J♣T♦) has 8 clean outs to make a straight (any A or 9) → ~31–32% to hit by the river. 32% is well below the ~40% required, so a straight call here is -EV. Raising as a semi‑bluff OOP is an option in some spots but here it’s unattractive: I’m out of position, Elon’s sizing is large (2/3 pot) and he’s short‑stacked enough to force an all‑in or a reshove I don’t want to face with only a draw; Flash’s check doesn’t guarantee he’ll fold to my raise. Given the pot odds, my equity, and stack dynamics, the correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I have 8-high on a board of Qd-Kh-5s with no draws and no pair. The pre-flop raiser, Elon, has bet 600 into a 900 pot, and he only has 1000 chips remaining behind. Given that I have no equity in this hand and the short stack is showing significant strength (or is committed), there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

K3d on the button is marginal but playable in position with a backdoor flush and king-high potential. Elon (BB) is very short (~24bb) — any open raise here is likely to induce a shove and I don’t want to get into a big coinflip with K3. Flash (SB) is deep and will defend widely, so a raise often just creates a bigger pot OOP or a tough shove decision. Calling 100 is cheap to see the flop, preserves fold equity postflop, and lets me leverage position: fold to heavy aggression if I miss, or extract value/raise if I hit a king or diamond draw.

I am in the Small Blind with Ah3c. Minni, on the Button, has just limped for 100. Elon in the Big Blind is extremely short-stacked with only 2400 chips (24 big blinds). While A3o is a decent hand, raising into a limp from a large stack like Minni while there's a short stack behind me who might shove is risky. However, in a 3-handed game, A3o is ahead of a button limping range. I will complete the small blind to see a flop cheaply, keeping the pot small and maintaining flexibility against Minni's large stack and Elon's potential shove.

Preflop in the big blind with Ks6d offsuit, facing limps from SB and Button creating a 300 pot for my 100 investment. Great implied odds to check and realize equity multiway on favorable flops. K6o has decent playability postflop, especially with position on SB, but too weak and offsuit to raise thin against deep stacks. Raising risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand; checking controls pot and sees flop cheaply. Opponents' limps suggest speculative/weak ranges; will reassess postflop.

The flop is extremely coordinated (7h-8d-Th), providing many straight and two-pair possibilities for my opponents. My hand is currently just Ace-high, although I do hold the Ace of hearts, which serves as the nut flush blocker and gives me a backdoor flush draw. Given that there are two opponents and the board hits a wide range of limping/checking hands, checking is the most prudent play. I want to see how the button (Minni) and the short-stacked big blind (Elon) react to this texture before committing any more chips.

Flop of 7h-8d-Th is fairly coordinated, hitting limpers' wide ranges with pairs, straights (e.g., 6-9, 9-J), two-tone straight/flush draws. My Ks6d is king-high with a weak gutshot straight draw (needing a 9 for 6-7-8-9-T, ~8 outs or 17% equity heads-up, less multiway). No pair or strong draw, and rainbow-ish board reduces flush immediate threats but backdoor possible. SB (Flash) checked, suggesting weakness or trap, but limpers often check weak made hands or draws. Pot is 300 (24BB for me at 2400 stack), deep effective stacks. Betting risks bloating pot OOP with marginal equity (~20-25% multiway) and low fold equity against speculative ranges; vulnerable to floats/raises from Button. Checking controls pot size, realizes cheap equity on favorable turn cards (9x best, but also broadway/K), induces bluffs from missed draws on later streets, and keeps pot multiway where my live king blocker and playability shine postflop. Consistent with preflop passive line on offsuit marginal hand.

Board is 7h 8d Th; I hold Kd3d — one overcard and a backdoor diamond draw. Both blinds checked, which usually indicates weakness or missed equity. My absolute hand equity is low (only ~12.5% to pair the K by river and ~4% to make a backdoor diamond flush), so I want fold equity rather than bloat the pot with a marginal hand. A small C-bet (~1/3 pot) exploits the checked ranges, picks up many single-pair and air hands, and keeps the pot manageable if I face resistance (Elon is short-stack ~24bb and Flash defends wide). Plan: bet small to take it down or see a cheap turn; fold to substantial raise/shove. If called, re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously.

The flop of 7h-8d-Th is very coordinated and likely hit the ranges of both the button limper (Minni) and the big blind (Elon). My Ah3c is only Ace-high with very weak backdoor possibilities. Minni's 100-chip bet is small, but even with 4:1 pot odds, calling out of position on this board texture is risky, especially since Elon is still to act behind me and could easily check-raise with his short stack. Ace-high has very little showdown value here, and many turn cards make the board even more dangerous. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

Facing a small 100 bet into 400 pot from Button after limping preflop, getting over 4:1 direct odds plus implied. Ks6d has king-high (live blocker to broadway), gutshot straight draw (4 outs to 9x for open-ender, ~8%), and backdoor diamond flush draw (board 8d, my 6d). Equity ~25-35% vs Button's polarized range: value (pairs like 77-TT, 87s, sets unlikely) or semi-bluffs/draws (J9 OESD, 65 str draw, heart flush draws like J9h/Q9h, 56). Button's limp-preflop followed by flop donk-bet into two checkers suggests capping weak pairs/draws or thin value, but wide enough that my hand has decent playability heads-up deep. OOP, but calling keeps pot small (540 post-call), controls size, realizes equity on 9x/royal cards/Kx turns (~20% hit rate), and can fold to strong turn aggression if missed. Raising semi-bluff risks bloating pot OOP with marginal equity (~30% vs calling range) against deep villain who may flat or 3-bet; fold equity low vs donk-bettor. Consistent with preflop/flop passive line to pot control speculative hand. History shows Minni mixes limps with aggression postflop, often value-heavy but folds to pressure sometimes (e.g., Hand 47 check-around). Call extracts value from worse and sets up fold equity later.

Turn board brings Ad, putting two diamonds on board (8d Ad), ace high, possible flush draws, top pair for broadway hands, completing some straight draws (56 for str). My Ks6d remains king-high with gutshot straight draw to 9 (~4 outs, 8%), backdoor heart? but primarily live K blocker blocking her AK/AQ top pair. Equity ~25-30% vs her flop donk-call range: weak pairs (77-99,87s), draws (J9 OESD, 65 str draw, hSD like 95h), some floats; ace likely improves her (A7-A9, A6 str draw). Heads-up OOP first to act into 500 pot (effective ~23BB, deep), betting thin value/semi-bluff risks raising into Ax/set/draw raise, low fold equity vs capping range that donk small flop. Checking controls pot (already invested 200 total), induces bluffs/checks from missed draws/weak pairs, realizes equity cheaply on river 9x/Kx (~17% combined), consistent with passive pre/flop line on speculative offsuit K6o. History: Minni button limper often aggro postflop but small sizes suggest weakness (e.g., Hand 47 check-raise with draws). Check to pot control and extract max from worse.

The turn A♦ changes the dynamic — it’s a scary card that improves a lot of hands in Elon's calling range and it also gives me a four-card diamond draw. I currently have K♦3♦ with 9 diamond outs + 3 kings = ~12 outs (~26% to improve on the river). Elon is short (~2,300) and can commit his stack; if I bet now I risk facing an all‑in where my ~26% equity is unlikely to justify a big call. I’m in position, so the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and realize my equity for one more card: if Elon bets the river I can evaluate pot odds and fold to too-large shoves, or call/follow up if the price or card is right; if he checks I see a free river and can decide whether to bluff or value-bet then. Action: check to control pot size and avoid being forced to call a costly shove on a marginal draw.

River 9s perfectly completes my gutshot straight draw from flop/turn, giving me 6-7-8-9-10 (second nuts behind only Broadway 10-J-Q-K-A, which my Ks blocks some combos). Board pairs no cards, adds spade filler, no flush completes (two hearts, two diamonds unimproved). Heads-up OOP vs Minni's button limp-call range (weak Tx/9x/8x/7x pairs, 87s two-pair, J9/56 OESD/semi-bluffs that donk flop small). Her flop donk 100 into 300 (passive/weak), check behind turn Ad (scared or capped), suggests no Ax/top pair strong, likely middle pair/draw. Pot 500, my 2300 stack (~23BB eff), deep opponent 24k. Value bet thin OOP to extract from weaker pairs (Tx+/87/98/76), missed draws inducing calls, or worse straights like 5-6-7-8-9. Half-pot 250 risks check-behind or small raise, but pot control not needed with nuts-ish hand; bet 350 (~70% pot) builds pot, gets value from 1-2 streets' worth if she flats (history shows she calls stations sometimes, e.g., Hand 47/50). Raises unlikely from value (sets rare, Broadway blocked), fold to jam if needed but low risk. Consistent passive pre/flop/turn line disguises strength for max value.

I hold K♦3♦ on a 7♥ 8♦ T♥ A♦ 9♠ river — I missed the flush and have only king-high. Elon called my small flop bet, checked the turn, then fires 350 into a pot of 850. Calling 350 to win a 1,200 pot gives me ~29% pot‑odds (350/(850+350)=29.2%), but my showdown equity versus his realistic calling range (pairs: T/8/7, A, made straights/9s, and some thin value hands) is far below that. The line is much more consistent with value than a frequent bluff, and a raise risks a shove or a call from hands that crush K‑high. I checked the turn specifically to avoid getting forced into this exact, marginal river decision. Fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

I am on the button with King-Nine offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. Elon is relatively short-stacked at 27.5 BBs, while Minni has a massive stack. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300.

Q6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against a button raise range. Facing a raise to 300 from Flash (aggressive raiser per history), out of position in SB, pot odds of 250 to win 450 (1.8:1) require ~35% equity, which this hand doesn't have. No strong reason to 3-bet bluff as stacks are decent and hand is bad. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I’m in the big blind with A♠K♣ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, 200 to call). AKo is a clear 3‑bet candidate heads‑up — it has huge equity vs the opener’s range and wants fold equity/isolation. I’ll 3‑bet to a standard ~3x the open (900 total). That sizing is big enough to fold out a lot of speculative hands and medium pairs, but keeps post‑flop playability against a wide button range and leaves room to maneuver with deep stacks (I’ve already posted 100, so I’d be adding 800 more). If Flash 4‑bets large I’ll re‑evaluate (vs a very large, polarized 4‑bet I’ll be cautious; vs a smaller 4‑bet I can continue), but preflop the optimal, value‑and‑equity maximizing line is a 3‑bet to 900.

Minni has shown a consistent pattern of 3-betting to 900 when I open from the button. While I have the advantage of position, King-Nine offsuit is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a standard 3-betting range and lacks the post-flop playability of suited hands. Since I've already seen her play aggressively in large pots, I will fold here rather than calling with a weak hand that could lead to difficult decisions on later streets.

3-handed on the button pre-flop with Tc2s offsuit, one of the weakest hands possible. Pot has 150 (dead SB 50 + BB 100), to call 100 for 1.5:1 but hand unplayable multiway and poor equity heads-up vs BB calling range (~35% vs typical 20-30% range). Even with position and dead money, T2o lacks connectivity, high card strength, or suitedness for stealing or implied odds. BB likely checked, standard play is fold weak offsuit low cards from button in this spot to avoid tough post-flop situations, especially short-stacked at 27BB while opponents are deep. Preserve stack for better spots.

I'm on the button (small blind) heads-up with 6♠7♥ — a connected hand that plays very well in position. Pot is 150 and calling 50 is cheap but passive. I want to seize initiative, build the pot with position, and apply pressure on Flash's wide BB defending range. A standard 2x BB open to 200 (total) keeps my range wide and gives fold equity while still being small relative to our deep stacks. If Flash 3-bets large I'll re-evaluate (likely fold vs a heavy 3-bet), but if he calls I can continuation-bet favorable flops and use positional advantage. Raise to 200.

I am in the big blind holding 2d 3h, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni, the small blind and current chip leader, has raised to 200. Although it only costs me 100 more to call into a pot of 300 (getting 3-to-1 odds), this hand performs very poorly post-flop and out of position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I want to preserve my chips to find better spots, folding is the most prudent play here.

Button vs only the blinds — open-raise a wide range to use positional advantage and steal. J♠5♦ is marginal but playable as a button opener; calling 100 keeps it multiway (where this hand performs poorly). A standard 3x open to 300 applies pressure to the SB and isolates the short-stack BB (Elon, 2,650), giving me postflop position and fold equity. If Elon decides to shove his short stack, his range will be polarized and I will fold J5o; if I get called heads-up I can play postflop with position. Raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with K2s. Minni, who has been active and is the chip leader, raises to 300 from the Button. While K2s has some post-flop potential and suitedness, I am out of position against a strong player. Given the stack sizes (Elon in the Big Blind is very short at 2650), calling or raising here might invite a shove from Elon or put me in a difficult spot post-flop against Minni. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

T9o is a decent speculative hand with straight potential and some broadway potential. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive in late position throughout the history, my BB defense range should include this hand given the pot odds (pot 450, calling 200 more for ~2.25:1). Effective stack is ~26BB, but realizing equity OOP by calling is better than folding a playable hand or raising spewily with a marginal holding. Plan to play postflop cautiously, looking to flop big or float if texture favors.

On the flop of 4s-Td-6s, holding Tc9d for top pair weak kicker heads-up OOP vs Minni's button raise range. Board is very draw-heavy with spade flush draw and open-enders like 75, 53. Minni's range is wide (overpairs, Ax suited, broadways, suited connectors) and she can have better Tx (AJT+), sets, two-pair, or strong draws. Betting out risks getting raised off equity or check-raised; checking allows pot control, induces bluffs from missed draws, and realizes equity by check-calling if she bets small or check-raising monsters (which I don't have). Plan: check, call small bets if she shows weakness, fold to large bets given kicker and reverse implied odds.

Flop is 4♠-10♦-6♠; I have J♠5♦. I was the preflop opener and we're heads-up against a short-stacked big blind who just checked. My hand has an overcard (J) and a backdoor spade, and the flop is relatively dry — a small continuation bet should fold out a lot of his calling range (weak broadways, small pairs, floats) while keeping the pot controllable if called. I want to leverage my position and range advantage with a ~1/3-pot c-bet to take the pot now or get cheap info. If he check-raises or jams, I’ll fold this marginal hand; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and barrel only on favorable cards (spade/J/weak-card folds) or check to control the pot. Bet 200 into 650 (~30% pot).

Facing a small 200 bet into 850 (~1/4 pot) on a draw-heavy flop with top pair weak kicker OOP vs Minni's wide button range. Pot odds are excellent (4.25:1), and my hand has good equity realization against her bluffs, missed draws (spades, 75, 53), and thinner value like Ax or weaker pairs. Overpairs and better Tx in her range are possible but not crushing; folding wastes equity from BB defense range, raising risks getting check-raised or outdrawn OOP with marginal kicker and reverse implied odds. Calling maintains pot control, invites bluffs on turn, and keeps her guessing on paired boards or runner-runner threats. Plan: call, evaluate turn texture (avoid spade flush or straight cards like 5/7/A), check-call small or fold to pressure if overbet.

On the turn Kd, board is 4s-Td-6s-Kd. Holding Tc9d for top pair weak kicker OOP heads-up vs Minni's wide button range (overpairs, Kx, Ax suited, broadways, suited connectors, bluffs). The Kd brings possible Kx value (KQ, KJ, AT suited), broadway straight draws (QJ, AQ), but doesn't complete spades or obvious straights (75 still open-end, needs 3/5). My hand blocks some Tx but weak to better pairs/kickers and sets. Pot control is key OOP with marginal strength and reverse implied odds on short stack (~21BB effective). Betting risks check-raise from her strong hands/draws and folds out bluffs; checking induces from her wide range (missed draws, Ax, weaker pairs), allows check-call small bets (great pot odds), or check-fold to large pressure. From history, Minni continuation bets small but can value bet thin; plan to check-call <1/2 pot or fold larger, preserving stack for better spots over 150 hands.

Turn K♦ significantly reduces the equity and bluffing potential of my J♠5♦. I c-bet the flop and got called, so Elon’s range contains many Kx, 10x, slow-played pairs or medium pocket pairs that improve or are happy to see a K. With no real made hand or a realistic two-street bluff plan, betting risks a shove from this short-stacked caller that I should fold to. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize equity, and keeps the river option open to bluff only on a clean J or blank — fold to size if Elon leads out. Check to avoid unnecessary bloating and preserve stack for better spots.

River 7s completes the spade flush (4s-6s-7s) and low straight (4-5-6-7-T), making this an extremely scary board for top pair weak kicker (Tc9d) OOP vs Minni's wide button opening range, which includes flush draws (AsXs, KsXs, etc.), straight draws (75s, 53s, 85s), better Tx (TJs+, ATs), Kx (KJ+, KQ), sets, and overpairs. My hand blocks some Tx but has poor kicker, reverse implied odds, and is crushed by her value range. Preflop call and flop call were for pot odds with equity realization, but turn check-check suggested she missed Kx/draw improvement, yet river bombs her range massively. Betting thin risks check-raise from monsters/flushes/straights or calls from better pairs; folding equity low against player who checked turn (weak/missed draw but can trap). Checking maximizes EV: induces bluffs/value from missed draws/weak Ax/Kx, controls pot OOP, realizes showdown value if she checks behind (history shows she does with medium strength), and preserves ~21BB stack for better spots in 150-hand game. Superior to spewy betting on draw-completing river.

I was the button, opened and c-bet the flop (small) and got called. Turn K♦ killed my backdoor outs and I checked to control the pot; Elon checked back, showing passivity. The river 7♠ is a blank for J5o — I have no pair and didn’t complete a flush. Elon is short-stacked (~2250) and the pot is 1050; betting here risks facing a shove or a call from hands that already beat me (10x, small pairs, Kx) while a small bet will also get called by many of those hands. With essentially no showdown value and poor bluffing prospects (no two-street plan, weak blockers), the lowest-variance + EV line is to check and fold to any river bet, preserving stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Td 6s. Both opponents have posted their blinds. The current hand history shows Elon (Small Blind) has a relatively short stack compared to Minni (Big Blind). T6o is a very weak hand, and even though I'm in position, it's generally not worth playing against a potential defend from either blind, especially since Elon is short-stacked and might be more inclined to shove or play aggressively pre-flop. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid unnecessary losses with a marginal hand.

Preflop in SB vs BB, unopened pot after Flash folds. A8s is a strong suited ace, well ahead of BB calling range. Standard to raise for value, isolation, and fold equity. Minni defends wide based on history but still profitable. Raise to 300 total (3x BB), standard size. Pot will be 300 + her BB 100 already in, good spot. My stack 3250 allows flexibility postflop.

3♦2♣ is a junk hand: offsuit, unconnected, very poor postflop playability. Elon opened to 300 and I must call 200 into a 400 pot, so I need ~33% equity to justify a call — far more than this hand can realistically expect versus a standard raising range. Out of position, I’d face tough decisions on most flops and risk facing a shove from a 3k stack. A light 3-bet bluff is possible but thin and unnecessary with so many better spots to pick. Fold and preserve my stack for stronger hands/positions.

3-handed on the button with KTo offsuit after SB folds. Pot 150 including dead SB money, facing BB's 100. KTo plays well post-flop in position with king-high live cards and straight potential. Standard play is to raise for value and steal blinds/dead money. Raising to 3x BB (300) balances aggression, builds pot with good equity, and leverages position. Flash's stack is deep but my 3400 allows it. Calling or folding is too passive given position and hand strength.

Facing a 3bb open to 300 from the button with the big blind still to act, I'm in the small blind with T♥4♥. This is a marginal, easily dominated hand and I'll be out of position post‑flop. To call I must put in 250 into a ~450 pot (I need roughly >36% equity to justify a call), and with the BB behind the pot is likely to become multiway, which further reduces the playability of T4s. A light 3‑bet to isolate is an option against a wide button range, but I want to reserve 3‑bets for hands that have better post‑flop playability or useful blockers (A/K/high cards) — T4s is not one of them. With Elon able to call or shove (≈31bb) and Flash showing willingness to defend, the simplest +EV line is to release this weak SB hand and wait for a better spot. Fold and preserve my deeper stack.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind. Elon, the button, has raised to 300. He is playing with a relatively short stack of 3,100 (31 BB). Against a standard button opening range, AT is a strong hand. I will 3-bet to 900 to exert pressure on the short stack and potentially take the pot down pre-flop. If Elon shoves, I will likely have the odds to call given the stack sizes.

Facing a 3-bet from BB to 900 after my button open to 300, with KTo offsuit in position. Pot 1250, to call 600 more, leaving me with ~2500 behind for SPR of ~1.35 postflop. KTo has decent equity against BB's 3-bet range (polarized between value like QQ+/AK and bluffs), with the king blocker reducing AA/AK likelihood and good straight/flush potential. History shows Flash plays aggressively pre and postflop, often bluffing, so their 3-bet range may be wide. Calling leverages position to realize equity in low SPR spots where I can outplay postflop. 4-betting risks too much with 31BB stack and marginal blockers; folding is too tight given dynamics. Standard exploitative call to balance range and maximize EV.

On the flop of Kd-9d-5h, I have Ace-high with one overcard to the second pair and no direct draw besides a backdoor straight draw. Elon, the button, called my pre-flop 3-bet and has about 2500 chips remaining (a pot-to-stack ratio of about 1.35). This board texture is generally better for the pre-flop caller's range (which includes hands like Kx, 99, 55, and various diamond draws). However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I still have a range advantage including AA, KK, and AK. Given the stack sizes, a check is the most prudent play. It allows me to see how Elon reacts. If he checks back, I can potentially see a free turn. If he bets, I have a difficult decision but can potentially fold and preserve my large stack, as I don't have enough equity to check-call a significant bet on this board.

Flop of 9d Kd 5h gives me top pair top kicker (Ks) with a Ten kicker, blocking some Ax hands and having straight draw potential with T (e.g., JQ). Board is draw-heavy with diamond flush draw and possible gutshots (67, 78, TJ). Flash 3-bet preflop from BB (aggressive history, wide 3-bet range including bluffs/polarized), then checks, which is weak/tempered strength or trap/inducing. In position with 2500 stack (pot 1850, SPR ~1.35 effective), betting extracts value from worse pairs/draws (e.g., 9x, flush draws, weaker Kx), protects vs. equity realization, and denies equity to floats. Check-back risks giving free cards to draws or allowing bluffs. Bet sizing ~2/3 pot (1200) builds pot while leaving low SPR (~0.4 post-bet) for committed lines, exploits Flash's aggression by polarizing my range (value/monsters). Consistent with calling 3-bet plan to realize equity postflop.

Elon's bet of 1,200 into a pot of 1,850 on a board of Kd-9d-5h is a strong commitment of his remaining stack (1,300 left). After calling my 3-bet pre-flop, his range is weighted toward mid-to-high pairs and broadway cards. My Ace-Ten high has very little equity against his likely range, which includes many Kings, Nines, and diamond draws. Given the pot-to-stack ratio, he is effectively all-in if I continue. Since I have no made hand and no significant draw, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chip lead.

I'm on the button with Q5s — a suited queen with good post‑flop playability and plenty of fold equity from position. With only the blinds to act (SB Flash ~118bb, BB Elon ~42bb) this is a standard open-raise spot: I want to steal the blinds and play the pot in position rather than limp. A 3xBB open to 300 is a table‑standard sizing (big enough to generate folds and deter cheap multiway calls, but not so large it forces unnecessary all‑ins vs the shorter BB). If I face a shove from Elon I will fold since Q5s is often dominated by a short‑stack shove range; if called I can leverage position and the suited nature of the hand. Raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s 5h, a weak suited-gapper. Minni, who has the largest stack at the table, has raised to 3x from the Button. While I have a decent stack, this hand is too weak to play out of position against a dominant stack, especially with Elon in the Big Blind who might also defend or jam. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chips for better spots.

4c9d is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability OOP against a button raise from Minni, who is aggressive but likely has a wide range. Pot odds are 450:200 (~3.25:1), requiring ~23% equity, but 49o has low equity and gets dominated often. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. No value in calling or raising.

I am on the button with Ad6d, a strong suited ace. This is a standard raising hand from late position to steal the blinds or play a pot with initiative and position. A raise to 300 is consistent with my previous sizing and puts pressure on the blinds.

Jd6h is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against a button raise range. Flash's button raise is likely wide, but J6o doesn't defend well from the small blind. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (calling 250 into 450), but the hand performs poorly postflop and isn't worth the risk with 42BB stack. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

I'm on the big blind with J♠6♠ facing a button open to 300; SB folded so it's heads-up. Pot is 450 and I need to add 200 (300 total) to call. Effective stacks are deep (Flash ~11,500, me 23,850) so implied odds and postflop playability matter. J6s has decent equity and backdoor flush/straight potential against a wide BTN opening range; calling requires ~30.8% equity (200 to win 650), which this hand can achieve. A tiny 3-bet to 500 wouldn't generate meaningful fold equity and would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand, while a larger 3-bet is unnecessary. I'll call to see a flop and proceed based on texture — continue when I hit or have strong draws, fold when clearly behind.

Heads-up out of position with J♠6♠ on 2c‑2s‑7h after I called the button open. I have no pair and only 6 outs to pair (≈12.8% on the turn, ≈24.1% by river) plus a very small (~4%) runner‑runner spade flush chance — overall very marginal equity. Flash checked the flop, which narrows his range to hands that will often call a c‑bet (pocket pairs, some overcards) or are trapping. Betting OOP with this hand bloats the pot with little fold or value equity and is exploitable. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and to realize equity. Plan: check and evaluate the turn. If he fires a small bet (~≤1/3–1/2 pot) I’ll call to see my outs; if he barrels large I’ll fold. If he checks back I’ll reassess on turn and look for a clean equity pickup or a spot to bluff later.

I have Ad6d on a 2c-2s-7h board. I was the pre-flop aggressor. This board is very dry and favors my range as the button opener. Minni checked to me. A continuation bet here is standard to take down the pot immediately, as it is unlikely Minni hit this board hard unless she has a 7 or a pocket pair. I'll bet roughly half the pot to keep the pressure on.

Call 325. Flash’s flop stab is small (≈1/3 pot), which gives me ~25% break‑even on a call. J♠6♠ has ~6 clean outs to pair (~24% to hit by river) plus a small (~4%) runner‑runner spade flush chance, so my effective equity is around or just above the required pot odds. Button c‑betting this size from a wide opening range is consistent with many bluffs and floats as well as some small pairs — calling to realize equity is the +EV line. I’m out of position and the hand plays poorly as a bluff‑raise, so folding or raising here would be too passive/aggressive respectively. Plan: see the turn — if I pair a J or 6 I’ll extract value; if I miss and he fires small again I’ll often call to see the river; if he barrels large I’ll fold.

Turn checked to me OOP on 2♣‑2♠‑7♥‑5♣ with J♠6♠. I haven’t improved: the backdoor spade chance is dead (turn was a club) and my only clean outs are the three Jacks and three Sixes (6 outs), ~13% to hit the river. Pot is 1,300 and there’s nothing to call now. Flash’s small flop c‑bet and turn check represent a wide range (floats, overcards, some small pairs); betting out here with ~13% equity would bloat the pot with very little fold or value equity and is likely -EV. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and to realize my equity rather than bluffing in position with a weak hand. I’ll check and see the river: if I pair a J or 6 I’ll extract value; if he bets a smallish amount on the river I’ll call to realize my outs, and I’ll fold to large river aggression unless I improve.

On the turn card 5c, the board remains very dry. I still have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw potential (though only one card left) and two overcards to the 7. Minni has checked again. Since I am the pre-flop aggressor and have already continuation-bet the flop, checking back here allows me to realize my equity and potentially see a cheap showdown or induce a bluff on the river. Betting again might only get called by better hands (like a 7, a small pocket pair, or a slow-played deuce) and doesn't accomplish much against her folding range which I already beat.

I just paired my Jack on the river (board: 2♣ 2♠ 7♥ 5♣ J♥) giving me top pair with a mediocre kicker. Flash opened preflop, made a flop c-bet and I called, then both of us checked the turn — that line polarizes his range toward both some value hands (small/medium pairs, possibly a disguised 2) and a lot of missed overcards/floatish holdings. A small-to-medium river value bet is the +EV play: it extracts thin calls from worse hands (A‑x, K‑x, Q‑x, 7x and lower pocket pairs) while keeping the pot controllable if I’m raised. I don’t want to overinflate the pot with just one pair on a paired board where trips are possible, so I’ll bet about one‑third of the pot to get thin value and fold to significant aggression. Bet 450 chips (~35% pot). If Flash raises big, I’ll fold; if he calls, I expect to win a lot of the time at showdown.

On the river Jack of Hearts, the board texture is 2-2-7-5-J. Minni leads for 450 into a 1300 pot (roughly 1/3 pot) after I checked back the turn. This lead often represents a marginal made hand like a 7, a small pocket pair, or a Jack that just improved on the river. While the pot odds are attractive (calling 450 to win 1750), my Ace-high has very little showdown value against a betting range. It is unlikely she is bluffing with a hand weaker than Ace-high, and most of her 'bluffs' or thin value bets (like a 5 or a 7) currently beat me. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Q6 suited on the button is a strong speculative hand with good playability for flushes and straights. Facing what appears to be a limp or small bet (pot 150, to call 100), position is excellent. Opponents have shown loose tendencies in history but fold to aggression often. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) aims to steal the pot preflop or build it with initiative postflop. Min raise is 200, but 300 is standard sizing for value/steal. Stack depth (42BB) supports this without overcommitting. Folding weak, calling multiway meh with position.

Elon opened to 300 from the button; I’m in the small blind with T♠5♠. This is a speculative, suited hand with decent playability — it can hit top pair, two-pair/straights occasionally and has a backdoor/flush potential. Folding is too tight given the price and my deep stack; 3-betting OOP with a hand like T5s is suboptimal (I’d rather conserve fold equity and avoid playing a big pot out-of-position). Calling 250 more (300 total committed) gives ~36% pot odds to see a flop and lets me realize equity and play postflop hands selectively. Plan: call to see the flop; continue on spade/ten/strong draw flops, otherwise play cautiously and fold to significant pressure out-of-position or against a 3-bet preflop.

I am in the big blind with 8c 4s. Elon raised from the button to 300, and Minni called from the small blind. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 to call. While the pot odds are roughly 3.5:1, 84o is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability and equity against typical button raising and small blind calling ranges. Furthermore, I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position. It's better to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot than to invest chips in a multi-way pot with a bottom-tier hand.

Check. I’ve completely missed this A♣ Q♥ 4♦ flop with T♠5♠ — no pair, no immediate pair-and only a weak backdoor spade flush (needs both turn and river). Elon was the preflop raiser and will c-bet a high frequency on this dry board, and his effective stack (~3,900) makes a shove or large turn/river bet a real possibility. Betting out OOP with air here is low EV: it risks a shove or commits chips when I have virtually no fold equity or showdown value. I’ll check to control the pot and see his action. Plan: if he checks back I get a free turn to improve; if he bets a small probe (~≤1/3 pot, ~≤230) I may consider a call as a cheap float only if I think I can apply pressure later, but I will fold to significant pressure (pot-sized bets or shoves) since my hand has poor equity.

Preflop raise with Q6s suited from button got a call from Minni in SB and Flash folded. Flop Ac Qh 4d gives me top pair Queens with Qd, plus a powerful diamond flush draw (3 diamonds to nut flush draw, 12 outs ~42% equity standalone). Board is somewhat dry but ace-high scares some hands. Minni checks, so I have initiative in position. Betting extracts value from her Ax weak, worse pairs (KJ, Qx), draws (diamonds, ST draws like 23o, 35o), and weaker holdings while building pot with massive equity. Half-pot bet ~350 sizes well for value/protection without overbetting vs huge stacks. Checking risks giving free cards to her draws or letting her realize equity cheaply. Strong spot to bet.

I completely missed this A♣ Q♥ 4♦ flop with T♠5♠ — no pair and only a very weak backdoor spade plan. Elon (the preflop raiser) bets 350 into the pot (pot now 1050). Calling 350 would make the pot ~1400 and requires roughly 25% equity to justify the call. Versus a standard button c-bet range (lots of A‑x, Q‑x and broadways), my hand’s equity is well below that (single digits to low teens). I’m out of position and will face uncomfortable turn/river decisions and likely further pressure from his deep stack. I already planned to check this flop and fold to meaningful pressure with no pair/draw. This 0.5‑pot continuation bet is large enough to qualify as that pressure — folding preserves chips and waits for a much better spot.

On the button with pocket 5s in a 3-handed pot I want to take the initiative and open a reasonably sized raise. Pocket fives play well as an opener — they have set-mining value with deep stacks but also deserve fold equity to pick up the blinds or isolate the BB. Elon (BB) has ~45bb and Flash (SB) ~110bb, and my stack is very deep, so I choose a standard 3xBB open to balance fold equity and implied odds. This size (300) is large enough to discourage too many speculative hands and small enough to avoid committing too much if I face a big 3-bet; I will re-evaluate if a sizable 3-bet arrives.

I am in the Small Blind with Q2s. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I have a queen, Q2o is a weak hand to play out of position against a loose and aggressive player like Minni, who currently holds the largest stack. Elon is in the Big Blind with a relatively short stack of 4500, which might prompt him to shove if I call. Given the positional disadvantage and the weakness of my hand, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

10-4 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against a button raise from an aggressive player like Minni. Pot odds are 200 to call into 450 (2.25:1), but my hand performs poorly postflop in position out of position. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 5d 3d. This is a speculative suited connector (gapped). With an effective stack of about 44BB (Elon has 4450), I can profitably open from the button to put pressure on the blinds and realize equity if called. A standard raise to 2.5x-3x is appropriate here.

97o is a trash hand with poor playability, low equity against a button steal-raise range from Flash, who opens frequently. SB defense should be wider than MP but this hand doesn't make the cut - no straight potential, dominated vs any calling range, bad postflop prospects. Pot odds are 250 into 450 (~2:1) but implied odds poor due to reverse domination risk and my short stack (44BB). Preserve stack for better spots.

Fold. 4♦6♥ offsuit is a very weak, poorly-connected hand and I’m out of position against a Button open. It costs me 200 more to call into a 450 pot (roughly 31% equity needed), and versus a typical button opening range my effective equity is lower and often dominated by A‑high and broadways. Postflop will be difficult to play OOP and there’s no compelling blocker or redraw to justify a light 3‑bet. Preserve the deep stack and wait for a better spot.

AJo is a strong hand on the button in a limped pot (pot 150, to call 100, likely blinds posted with no prior raise). Raising builds the pot for value against wide calling ranges from blinds, steals dead money, and secures positional advantage postflop. Standard sizing: 3x the current bet to 300. Effective stacks allow it; opponents have shown wide ranges in history.

I’m in the small blind with 7♥4♠ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call (I’d need ~35.7% equity to break even: 250/(450+250)). 7-4o is a very weak, easily dominated hand and I’ll be out of position postflop vs both opponents. A call is a poor long-term +EV decision (and I could face multiway action if the big blind defends); a raise is also inappropriate as a bluff here given stack sizes and likely 3-bets. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the big blind with a pocket pair of sixes (6c 6h). Elon, on the button, has raised to 300. With a pocket pair, I have a solid hand to see a flop. Calling is standard here to set-mine and see how the board develops, especially since Elon's stack is relatively short (4150 chips). I have plenty of chips to call the additional 200.

I have a pocket pair of sixes, which is an underpair to the King and Nine on the board. On this dry K-high board, the advantage lies with the pre-flop aggressor, Elon. My goal is to reach showdown cheaply or fold if the pressure becomes too high, as my hand is unlikely to improve. I will check to Elon to see how he reacts to this flop. Leading here would likely only be called by better hands and fold out hands I currently beat.

Heads-up on the flop with AsJd facing a check from Flash in the BB after calling my button raise pre-flop. The board is 2s Ks 9h, giving me the nut flush draw (10 outs) plus backdoor straight potential with J. No pair, but strong draw equity (~35-40% vs random). Checked to me in position, pot 650. Opponent's check suggests weakness or trapping, but history shows Flash calls wide pre but folds to aggression often post-flop (e.g., Hands 41,46,60). C-bet for value against weak Ax/Kx pairs/draws, fold equity, build pot, deny free cards. Sizing 2/3 pot (~430, round to 400) balances range including sets/top pair/overpairs. Effective stack allows; protects against villainous draws/check-raises.

I have a pocket pair of sixes, which is currently an underpair to the King and Nine on the flop. Elon, as the pre-flop aggressor, has a range advantage on this King-high board. However, his bet of 400 into 650 is a standard continuation bet that he could make with many hands I currently beat, such as smaller pairs or high-card air (AQ, AJ, etc.). I am getting reasonable pot odds to call and see how the turn develops. I will call this bet but remain cautious, as many turn cards could improve his range or make my hand difficult to play out of position.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Deuce of Hearts (2h). This card is generally a blank unless the opponent has an unlikely Deuce in their range. As the caller out of position, I will continue to check to the pre-flop aggressor. My pocket sixes are currently an underpair to the King and Nine, so I am looking to reach a cheap showdown or potentially fold if Elon continues to show significant strength with a large bet, especially since his stack is relatively shallow.

On the turn, checked to in position with the nut flush draw (As on a board with 2s Ks, 10 spade outs for the nuts) and backdoor straight potential. Pot is 1450, my stack 3750 allows for aggression. Flash called flop c-bet on K-high board but checks again, representing possible weakness like mid pairs, weaker Kx, or missed draws. History shows Flash folds to continued aggression post-flop frequently (e.g., Hands 41, 46, 60, 68). Semi-bluff bet sizes to 1000 (about 2/3 pot) for thin value against worse made hands/draws, high fold equity, denies free river cards, and builds pot if called for river overbet potential if spade hits. Folds to large raise/shove to preserve stack.

On the turn, the board pairs with the 2h. Elon has followed up his flop continuation bet with a larger second barrel of 1000 into a 1450 pot. My pocket sixes are currently an underpair to both the King and the Nine on the board. While Elon could be semi-bluffing with a spade draw or total air, his sizing and the commitment of a large portion of his stack suggest significant strength, likely a King or a better pocket pair. I am only beating absolute bluffs and very few draws, and I have very little equity to improve. Given the board texture and the betting action, it is best to fold and preserve my stack.

I'm on the button with KcTc (suited K-T) — a strong, positionally advantaged hand that plays well multi-street. It’s folded to me with the small and big blind to act; pot is 150 and my cost to see a flop is 100. I want to take the initiative, deny good pot odds to the blinds, and build a pot with a hand that has both showdown and bluffing potential. In this game the standard open from late position is ~3x the BB, so I’ll raise to 300. That sizing gives me fold equity versus the blinds, reduces multiway limp/call scenarios compared with a min-raise, and keeps postflop options open. Plan: continuation-bet favorable flops, play for value or pot-control on wetter or dominated boards, and re-evaluate vs a 3-bet (be prepared to fold to large, polarized 3-bets or call/4-bet selectively depending on opponent sizing and tendencies).

I am in the Small Blind with 9-5 offsuit, a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Given that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand and my hand has very poor equity and playability, the most disciplined and standard play is to fold.

9h3s is a complete trash hand offsuit with no connectivity or high cards. Facing a button raise from aggressive Minni, out of position in BB. Pot odds are ~2.25:1 (200 to call into 450 pot), but equity against her wide button range is abysmal (~20-25% at best). Folding preserves stack for premium spots. No history this hand justifies a defend.

I am on the button with Q9o in a 3-handed game. Q9o is a standard opening hand from the button to put pressure on the blinds. Given the stack sizes, specifically Elon's 50BB stack, a standard 3x raise is appropriate to attempt to win the blinds or play the hand in position.

67o is a marginal speculative hand at best, offsuit connectors with low cards. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has been aggressive in previous hands. As SB, I'm OOP post-flop, and likely to face multi-way action with Minni in BB who defends frequently. Pot odds are 450:250 (~1.8:1), but equity realization is poor OOP with this hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots. Deep stacks, no need to gamble here.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with QcTh (offsuit) and only need to put in 200 more to call. With deep stacks and heads-up vs a BTN open, QTo has reasonable equity and plays well postflop (can hit broadway pairs and good straight draws). Pot odds: calling 200 into a 450 pot gives me ~30.8% immediate pot odds (200/(450+200)), and QTo vs a standard BTN opening range meets or exceeds that equity often — plus there are healthy implied odds deep-stacked. 3-betting OOP with QTo is thin: I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position with a marginal hand or give up too much fold equity with only a small min-raise. Plan is to call 200 (total commitment 300) to see a flop, then play cautiously OOP: proceed aggressively only with strong pairs/clear draws, and fold/release to heavy action when I miss.

I have QcTh — top pair with a decent kicker on Tc-9d-8d. This is a very dynamic flop: connected and two diamonds, so there are many straight and diamond draws that can outdraw me on later streets. Checking gives the preflop aggressor free card equity and lets him take pot-control or barrel at me; betting extracts value from worse tens, 9s/8s that will call, and charges Jx/7x and single-diamond hands. I’ll make a protection/value bet of about half-pot to deny equity and keep worse hands/draws in. If Flash raises big, I’ll re-evaluate (small raise I’ll likely call and assess the turn; a large, polarized raise is often sets/two-pair and I’ll fold). Bet 325 into a 650 pot balances getting value and keeping fold equity against draws.

On a very coordinated board of Tc-8d-9d, I have middle pair (Nines) and a gutshot straight draw (Jack). Minni has led out with a donk bet of 325 into a 650 pot. My hand is too strong to fold given the pot odds and the fact that I have both a pair and a draw. However, raising on such a wet board is risky, as many turn cards could complete straights or flushes. Calling allows me to see a turn card in position and reassess the strength of my hand based on the next card and Minni's subsequent action.

The turn 9s pairs the board (Tc 9d 8d 9s) and significantly increases the number of hands that now beat me (any 9 in villain’s hand is trips; some T9/99/9T combos can already be ahead or are close to a full house). I currently have top pair (T) with a Q kicker — a decent but vulnerable holding, and I was the aggressor on the flop. Betting again OOP into a paired turn risks building a big pot against hands that have me crushed and can induce check‑raises from sets/trips; a bet would extract only from worse tens and some two‑pair/8x combos and would charge relatively few bluffs. With deep stacks and the button in position, pot control is the prudent line: check and see how Flash responds on the river. Plan: if he checks back we go to showdown; if he leads a small/medium bet on the river I will call for value with my kicker, but I will be inclined to fold to a large polarized river bet that represents trips/full house.

On the turn, the 9s improves my hand to trips (three of a kind). The board (Tc 8d 9d 9s) is highly coordinated with several straight possibilities (J7, QJ, 76) and a diamond flush draw. Minni donk-bet the flop and has now checked the turn. This check often indicates a marginal made hand (like a Ten or an Eight) or a draw that is slowing down. I should bet here to extract value from those weaker pairs and draws while also protecting my hand against potential river cards that could complete a straight or a flush. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot (800) is a standard size to build the pot while still being foldable if Minni check-raises significantly, which might indicate a completed straight.

I have top pair (T) with a Q kicker on Tc-8d-9d-9s. I checked the turn to control the pot — Flash's 800 is a modest, non‑polarized bet that can include both value (9x, T9, TT, 88, sets/two‑pair) and bluffs/semi‑bluffs (diamond draws, floats). Calling 800 into a 2100 pot gives ~28% immediate pot odds, and QTo has reasonable equity against a wide continuing range plus good implied odds with deep stacks. Raising OOP here is thin: it bloats the pot against many hands that already beat me and won't fold out the worst value hands, while folding gives up too much. I will call 800 to keep the pot manageable and get to the river, then re‑evaluate: call small/medium river bets for showdown value, but fold to large polarized river aggression that represents trips/full house.

I have QcTh and the final board is Tc 8d 9d 9s 9c — I make a very strong hand: nines full of tens (999TT). Hands that beat me are rare: any 9 in villain’s hand (quads) or pocket tens (TT -> TTT99). Given Flash’s line (BTN open, called my flop raise, then bet the paired turn and I called) his continuing range contains some tens, some 9-combos, medium pocket pairs and missed diamond draws. The combos that actually beat me (a single 9 or pocket tens) are a small subset of that range, while many of his continuing hands (88, other pocket pairs, Kx/Qx, missed draws, single-T hands that would tie) are behind or will pay me off.

I’m first to act on the river with a very strong made hand and want to extract value without giving too much fold equity to bluffs that I already beat. A medium‑large value bet (~60% pot) prices in calls from worse pocket pairs and stubborn broadway/one‑pair hands and keeps the pot manageable if I get a raise (a large raise would be polarized and usually indicate quads or TT). Bet 1800 into 2900: this size balances getting called by worse hands while not overcommitting to an unlikely check‑raise monster. If Flash jams big, I’ll re‑evaluate (such a raise is more often quads or TT which beat me).

I have the absolute nuts. The board is Tc-8d-9d-9s-9c, and I hold the 9h. This gives me four-of-a-kind (quads). Since there are only four nines in the deck and I hold one while three are on the board, it is impossible for any other player to have quads. The best hand any opponent can have is a full house (such as TT-999 or 88-999). Minni has led for 1800 into a pot of 2900, which indicates she likely has a very strong hand like a full house or is attempting a large bluff. To maximize value from her full houses, I will raise all-in.

I have QcTh on Tc-8d-9d-9s-9c: that’s a full house (999TT). I bet 1,800 and Flash jammed/raised to 9,000 (I must call another 7,200). Pot is 13,700; calling 7,200 would make the final pot ~20,900 so my break-even equity on a call is ~34.5% (7,200 / 20,900). Hands that actually beat me are rare: any hand with the lone remaining 9 (quads), the one combo of pocket tens (TsTd), and a few overpairs (JJ+, which are only a handful of combos). Unweighted combinatorics put those losing combos well below the 34% threshold, and villain’s line also allows missed diamond bluffs and single-T or weaker pairs that will pay me off. Given the huge positive EV of calling versus the small chance I’m crushed (quads/TT/very rare overpairs), I will call to get to showdown and extract value from worse holdings and bluffs. I commit to calling the additional 7,200 (total 9,000 committed on the river).

AhTh is a premium opening hand from the button in a 3-handed pot. Excellent position on both opponents, suited for flush potential, ace-high with straight possibilities. Standard raise sizing to 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot while denying equity to worse hands and taking initiative preflop. Opponents have shown aggression in history but my position and hand strength justify raising over limping or calling.

I have 4♣7♦ in the small blind — a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor postflop playability. Elon raised to 300 (3x) from the button; the pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call. Calling would leave me out of position versus the button and likely face a defended multiway pot with Flash (deep), making postflop decisions difficult and +EV small. 3-betting as a bluff is not attractive with this holding. With a healthy 14k stack and many hands left, the correct play is to preserve chips and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 3c, which is a very weak hand. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. Although the pot odds are okay, this hand has extremely poor playability and equity against a standard Button opening range. Given the strength of my hand, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm on the button in a 3-handed pot with Qd9c — a playable, late-position hand. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call. Raising to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds is the +EV play: Q9o is in my 3-handed open-raise range and I can leverage position postflop. A standard 3x BB open to 300 balances fold equity and keeps the pot manageable against both a deep-stacked SB and a mid-stacked BB. Plan: if everyone folds I win the blinds; if I'm called I'll use position to C-bet favorable flops and fold to heavy, committed aggression when clearly behind. If I face a large, healthy 3-bet I will usually fold this hand preflop.

I have a premium starting hand with Ace-King of spades in the Small Blind. Minni has opened from the button for 300. With a very strong hand, I want to 3-bet to build the pot and gain initiative, especially since I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand. A 3-bet to 1,000 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the opener and potentially fold out the Big Blind.

6h Jc is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability out of position in the big blind. Facing a button open-raise to 300 and a small blind 3-bet to 1000, the range for Flash's 3-bet is likely strong, including pairs, broadway, and suited connectors. Minni may continue, leading to a bloated pot OOP with a speculative hand that rarely hits strongly. Pot odds are about 2.55:1 but not sufficient given the reverse implied odds and multiway risk. Folding preserves my 51bb stack for better spots.

In a 3‑handed spot Qd9c is worth playing because I have position and the stacks are deep. Flash’s 3‑bet to 1000 is a standard, somewhat small sizing — he can have a wideish, polarized range, but I’m not comfortable 4‑betting Q9o as a bluff and folding is too nitty given position. Calling 700 more (1000 total) keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity and play post‑flop with position. Postflop plan: continue aggressively when I connect or have good equity/draws, exercise pot control and fold to heavy commitment when clearly behind; if Flash 4‑bets shove I’ll fold.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-King of spades and was called by Minni. The flop comes 7-7-8, which is a relatively dry, paired board. While this board doesn't hit my specific hand, I maintain a range advantage as the 3-better, holding all the high pocket pairs (AA-99). I will lead out with a continuation bet of approximately 1/3 the pot to maintain initiative, extract value from worse high-card hands, and put pressure on her medium-strength holdings.

Call. I called Flash's 3-bet preflop intending to play post‑flop in position; this flop (7h 7d 8s) misses Q9o but is fairly dry and Flash's continuation bet is very small (700 into a 2800 pot). That sizing is consistent with a wide/probing range and gives me excellent pot odds to see a turn — calling 700 (I’ve already committed 1000 preflop) makes my total commitment 1700 and costs a small fraction of my 13k stack. I have ~6 clean outs to a pair (roughly ~24% to hit by the river) and need only ~16.7% equity to break even on this call, so folding would be too nitty and raising as a bluff is too thin with no real equity or backdoor draw. Plan: call to realize equity and use my position to pressure on a favorable turn or fold to clear, heavy turn aggression; if I pair Q or 9 I will extract value, if villain checks turn I may take the initiative.

The turn is the 6d, making the board 7h-7d-8s-6d. This board texture is becoming increasingly coordinated and favors the caller's range (Minni), which contains more middle connectors (like 98, 86, 54, 97s) than my 3-betting range. Although I have overcards and a backdoor spade draw, I am currently unimproved. Checking here is the most prudent play: it allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free river, or evaluate based on Minni's betting sizing if she chooses to lead. It also protects my checking range since I still have plenty of high-equity hands and occasional 7x in this spot.

Situation: heads-up after SB (Flash) checked the turn on 7h 7d 8s 6d. I hold Qd9c, pot = 3,500, my stack = 12,300. Assessment: my hand has missed but has ~10 clean outs to improve to at least a pair/straight on the river (3 Qs + 3 9s + 4 fives ≈ 10/46 ≈ 21.7%). Flash 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet small on the flop, then checked the turn — a line consistent with a fairly wide, somewhat polarized range that contains a lot of missed overcards and medium hands that will fold to pressure. Plan: convert fold equity now with a small, non‑committal stab rather than giving a free river. A bet of ~1/3 pot (1,200) requires only ~25.5% fold frequency to be break‑even (1,200/(3,500+1,200)), which is realistic versus his checked range. Action plan: bet 1,200 to steal the pot when he is weak, keep the sizing small so I can fold to a strong check‑raise, and re‑evaluate if he calls (river decisions based on whether I hit). If he check‑raises I will fold — I won’t inflate the pot with a marginal hand.

On the turn, the board is 7h-7d-8s-6d. Minni has bet 1,200 into a pot of 3,500 after I checked. This sizing (approx 1/3 pot) is relatively small, giving me excellent pot odds of nearly 4:1 to call. While the 6d is not a great card for my range, Ace-King remains the best non-pair hand and has 6 clean outs to improve to top pair on the river, which would likely be the best hand. Additionally, Minni could be betting for protection with a pair of 8s or taking a stab with a draw like 9-10. Given the price, folding would be too exploitable and tight, while raising turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the river.

The river 9d completes a highly coordinated board (6-7-8-9) where any 5 or 10 makes a straight, and the board is already paired with 7s. My Ace-King has no showdown value against Minni's likely range, which consists of pocket pairs, suited connectors, or hands that hit this board. Having checked the turn and called a small bet, leading out now would be an expensive bluff into a range that has shown strength. I will check and evaluate, though I expect to fold to any significant bet as Ace-high is unlikely to be the best hand here.

I improved to a second pair on the river (Qd9c on 7h 7d 8s 6d 9d). Flash’s line — a preflop 3‑bet, a small flop c‑bet he got called, then checking the turn and checking the river — looks like a lot of medium/weak hands (88, 66, A/Kx, missed broadways) and some showdown‑type hands. My 9s beat most of that calling range and only lose to trips/quads (7x, 99) or a few unlikely combos. I want a small/medium value bet to extract from worse hands while keeping the price low if I’m behind; ~40% pot (~2,400) achieves that and only needs about ~29% fold frequency to be break‑even. I will bet 2,400 for value and fold to any river check‑raise (I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal holding).

The river card 9d completes a highly coordinated board (6-7-8-9 with a pair of 7s). My Ace-King has no showdown value, as even the smallest pairs in Minni's range (like 22-66) or hands that were draws on the turn (like 56 or 9T) have now improved to better than Ace-high. Minni's line of calling a 3-bet, calling the flop, and then betting both the turn and river is very strong. Although the pot odds are roughly 3.5:1, I am losing to her entire value range and even most of her 'missed' draws that might have accidentally paired. Folding is the most disciplined play.

I am on the button with King-Six offsuit. In a three-handed game, opening ranges from the button should be relatively wide. While King-Six is a marginal hand, it is strong enough to open for a steal or to play in position post-flop against the blinds. Elon in the small blind has a shorter stack (around 50BB), which makes him more likely to play 'push or fold', but I will stick to a standard open size of 3x the big blind to maintain pressure.

Pre-flop as SB facing a Button raise to 300 by Flash. My hand J7s is a suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential. Button ranges are wide (likely 30-50% of hands), and calling from SB gets good pot odds (250 to call into 450 pot, ~35% equity needed, J7s has ~40%+ vs wide range). Post-flop implied odds are strong deep-stacked. Folding too tight; 3-betting not ideal with this hand as it's better multiway or floating. Minni in BB may call/raise, but still +EV spot. Call to realize equity and see flop cheaply.

Facing a button open to 300 with the small blind calling, this is a three-way pot and I’m out of position. 9♦4♦ is a very weak, easily-dominated hand with poor postflop playability multiway. To call I must put in 200 into a 700 pot (≈22% break-even equity) — against a button open and an SB cold-caller my actual equity with 9-4s will normally be below that and I’ll rarely realize full equity in multiway pots. Raising to isolate would be reckless with this holding (poor fold equity and I’d be building a pot out of position versus a deeper, aggressive opener). With healthy stack depth and aggressive opponents, the correct long-term EV play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Flop: 8s 3s Jd gives me top pair (Jacks) with a weak 7 kicker on a coordinated, drawy board with two spades (flush draw possible) and gutshot possibilities. I called pre-flop from SB vs Button raise with J7s suited connector, realizing equity multiway or vs wide range. Now first to act with 4800 stack into 700 pot. Top pair weak kicker is vulnerable to better Js (AJ/KJ/QJ), overpairs (QQ+/AK), sets (JJ/88/33 unlikely), or strong draws (spade flush draws, str8 draws like 456/45x/9T). Betting thin risks getting raised/folded out by better hands or called by draws/overcards. Checking pot controls, induces bluffs from Flash's wide Button range (misses often, c-bets light), allows check-behind if checked to, or calling a bet with decent equity/showdown value. Deep stacks favor check-call/check-fold. History shows Flash aggressive on buttons/flops. Check to play post-flop optimally.

On this flop (Js 8s 3s), I have King-high. My opponent (Elon) checked to me. While I have two overcards, this board doesn't hit my range particularly well, and I don't have any spade. However, as the pre-flop aggressor in position, a continuation bet (C-bet) is standard to take down the pot immediately, especially since Elon has shown a tendency to play fairly straightforwardly. A small bet of roughly half the pot should be enough to test his interest in the hand.

Facing Flash's small c-bet of 350 into 1050 pot on a drawy flop (8s3sJd), offering excellent pot odds (~4:1 effective). Top pair J7cc (weak kicker) has good showdown value and equity vs Button's wide c-bet range (misses, weak pairs, draws like spades, gutshots 456/9T). My clubs provide backdoor flush/straight potential. Folding top pair too tight deepstacked; raising risks isolation with vulnerable hand (better Js, overpairs, sets/draws). Calling keeps pot controlled, induces bluffs on turns/brick rivers, exploits Flash's aggression/history of light betting. Plan: call, re-eval turn (check strong draws/combos or bet protection if checks back). Consistent with pre-flop call and flop check strategy to realize equity multiway/cheaply.

Turn board Ts completes three spades (heavy flush draw potential, e.g., any two spades including QsJs, Ks9s, etc.), improves spade straight draws (e.g., 456s, 9s for nut flush draw + str8), and pairs possible tens. My Jc7c top pair (Jd) weak kicker now highly vulnerable OOP to: flushes, better Js (AJs/KQs/QJs spades), sets (JJ/88/33/Ts unlikely pre but possible), two pairs (JTs/J8s/810s), strong draws.
