Pot 1400, stack 4450 (SPR ~3), acting first. Betting thin value/protection poor: induces raises from better hands/draws, gets called by worse but folds bluffs. Checking best: pot control, induces Flash's button-aggression (history shows frequent c-bets/continuation, e.g., hands 41,49,54,56,68,74 light betting/value thin), exploits wide range (misses, Ax overcards, weak 8x/3x/Tx, bluffs) for calls/folds profitably. If checks back, free river showdown value. Plan: check, call small-medium bets (odds >3:1, equity vs polarized range), fold to x2+ pot (polarizes to nuts), re-eval river. Passive OOP realization equity optimal vs deep aggro button, per preflop/flop play.

The board has become very dangerous with the Ten of Spades completing potential flushes and straight draws. My King-high has very little equity against Elon's flop-calling range. Checking back is the most prudent play to keep the pot small and see a river card for free, as another bet is unlikely to fold out better hands on such a coordinated texture.

River Jh pairs the Jack, giving me trip Jacks (Jc with Jd/Jh) with a 7 kicker, making this near-nuts on a board with three spades (8s/3s/Ts possible flush), limited straight potential (no obvious broadway or lower straights completed without specific combos like Q9), and unlikely full houses (e.g., TT for tens full unlikely from button range). Pre-flop button raise by Flash (wide ~40% range), flop c-bet 350 into 1050 (standard light c-bet/draw/value thin Jx/Ax/high cards), turn check-back (weak/missed draw or slowplay), now OOP first to act into 1400 pot with 4450 stack (SPR ~3). Checking risks free showdown (loses value from Ax high, smaller pairs like 88/TT/any T/8x, Jx worse kicker, missed spade draws turning into bluffs). Value betting exploits Flash's calling station tendencies (history: calls down light, e.g., Hand 17,39,44,47,52,55,74 with marginal made hands/draws), induces from overcards/bluffs. Sizing 900 (~2/3 pot) balances value/protection, gets called 1-2 streets by worse while folding out pure air if raised (though rare). Deep effective stacks but low SPR favors shoving value here vs aggro button. Optimal: bet for max EV.

On the river, the board has paired the Jack and completed the flush draw with the Ten of Spades appearing on the turn. My King-high has no showdown value against Elon's range, which likely includes a Jack, an Eight, or a flush. Elon's lead on the river for nearly 65% of the pot indicates strength or at least a hand better than mine. Calling with King-high is unprofitable in this spot.

Preflop on the button in an unraised pot (SB Minni limped, BB Flash checked). A4o plays well from this spot due to ace-high equity, position for postflop control, and ace blocker. Opponents' limping/checking ranges are wide and weak. Raising to 3x the BB (300) isolates, builds the pot, and applies pressure. Standard play maximizes EV long-term.

I'm in the small blind with K♥Q♠ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, to call 250). KQo is strong enough versus a wide BTN opening range to 3-bet for value and fold equity. I want to apply pressure and try to isolate Elon while discouraging the big blind from cold-calling; a 3-bet to ~1,200 is a standard, constructive size against his stack (~55BB) — big enough to get weaker broadways/Ax to fold or pay to see a showdown, but small enough that I can fold to a large 4-bet. If called I'll proceed cautiously out of position and look to realize equity or take initiative on favorable textures. Therefore I raise to 1,200.

I have Ten-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind. Elon opened to 3 big blinds and Minni 3-bet to 12 big blinds from the Small Blind. This hand is significantly below the threshold for continuing against a 3-bet. Folding is the only viable option here to preserve my stack.

Facing a 4x 3bet from Minni in the SB after my button open-raise to 300. As4o has poor equity against a strong SB 3betting range (QQ+, AKo, AQs+), roughly 25-30% at best, and limited playability postflop due to offsuit nature and weak kicker. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (900 to call into 1600), insufficient even with position and implied odds, as we rarely get stacks in profitably. 4betting has fold equity but As4o blocks AA/AKs poorly for bluffs and plays poorly postflop if called. Opponent tendencies show Minni is aggressive but SB 3bets are strong; folding preserves stack for better spots in a long session (78/150 hands). Standard discipline maximizes EV.

I'm on the button with A♥4♠ in a three-handed pot (Flash SB 171bb, Elon BB ~54bb). This is a clear spot to be aggressive and take the initiative — A4o is a playable button opener that blocks some strong ace combinations and plays well in position. I want to open to a standard 3x BB to get fold equity, isolate the shorter BB, and avoid a multiway pot where my weak kicker is a liability. If I face a large 3-bet/shove from Elon I will fold; versus a single call I'll play postflop in position (c-bet A-high/dry boards, pot-control on dangerous textures). Raising to 300 keeps the pot manageable while maximizing fold equity and postflop advantages.

I am in the Small Blind with 83o, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised from the button. Given the positional disadvantage and the weakness of my cards, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

AQs is a premium hand in the big blind facing a button raise from Minni, who has shown aggression from late position throughout the history. Button opening ranges are wide, and AQs dominates much of it (worse aces, broadway, suited connectors). Calling gives them position postflop and allows multiway pots if SB re-enters, but SB folded. 3-betting isolates, builds the pot with value, and applies pressure. Raising to 900 (3x the raise) is a standard size with deep stacks (effective ~5450). Pot odds are great even for a call, but 3-bet maximizes EV. No fold equity issues with this strength.

Elon (BB, ~46bb) 3-bet to 900 after my button open to 300. I’m going to call (total 900). Rationale: A♥4♠ is marginal vs a 3-bet but it plays very well IN POSITION and it removes a lot of strong A‑combos from his range. The pot is 1250 — calling 600 more to make it 1850 requires ≈32.4% equity, which A4o can achieve against a realistic BB 3‑bet range (pairs, broadways, suited Axs and some suited connectors), especially with position and blocker value. A small 4‑bet or shove is unnecessary and likely gets me into a dominated or all‑in spot versus a shorter stack; folding is too nitty given the price and my positional advantage. Postflop plan: c‑bet A‑high or very dry boards for value/protection; check and pot‑control on wet/connected or two‑tone boards and fold to heavy action if I’ve missed; call small turns with backdoor equity but fold to polarized shove unless I’ve improved. If Elon jams all‑in preflop, I’ll fold because I’m too often dominated.

Preflop 3-bet with AQs was standard for value and isolation against button open. Flop 2s6d2c is a complete miss: ace-high no pair, no draws, rainbow paired board caps my range low but strengthens villain's calling range (pairs like 22/66, overpairs QQ+, AK/AQ). Board is dry, low SPR (~2.5:1 with my 4650 stack into 1850 pot), no fold equity for a bluff as villain can call/raise wide with any pair or better ace/draw. Checking preserves stack, allows free card or villain's check-back, and sets up fold to bet/call-down thin. Betting air here loses value long-term against Minni's history of calling stations in multi-street pots.

I’m on the button with A♥4♠ in position vs Elon’s preflop 3‑bet. Flop is very dry and paired (2♠ 6♦ 2♣) and Elon checked — exactly the kind of board where a small continuation bet with an Ace blocker is profitable. My A blocks many of his A‑combos and a small c‑bet will fold out missed broadway and Kx hands that he 3‑bet light with, while keeping the pot manageable against his shorter stack. I’ll make a ~1/3 pot c‑bet (600) to take the pot down or get cheap information. If he jams or check‑raises all‑in I’ll fold — A4o is usually dominated versus a polarized check‑raise from his 3‑bet range. If he flat‑calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (pot‑control and fold to heavy action unless I improve).

Preflop 3-bet with AQs was correct for value/isolation. Flop 2s6d2c completely misses: ace-high, no pair/draw on paired dry board. As preflop aggressor checking is standard to pot control/realize equity. Minni's 600 bet (1/3 pot) into checked board after calling 3-bet from button reps value (22/66/sets/pocket pairs/draws like 54/A3 suited) over bluffs (overcards/gutshots unlikely given board texture capping bluffs). My range reps strength but this holding has zero showdown value and poor playability OOP. Low SPR (~2.5) post-call commits further without improvement path. History shows Minni calls stations but value bets thin here; no fold equity for raise, call induces turns where I fold to pressure. Stack preservation prioritizes: folding high card air maximizes long-term EV over 150 hands.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. Elon is the short stack and might shove, which I will likely call given the strength of my hand and the pot odds. Minni has been aggressive, so I'll be prepared for a 3-bet.

Q℃ 4♠ is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against a button raise. Facing a raise to 300 (3x BB) from Flash on the button, who has been aggressive in previous hands, and out of position in the small blind with Minni yet to act behind. Pot odds are 250 to call into 450 (about 1.8:1), but the hand doesn't justify it preflop. Folding preserves my 46BB stack for better spots.

Facing a button open to 300 from Flash while I'm in the BB with J♠4♦. Pot is 450 and I must call 200 more; calling 200 to win 650 requires ~30.8% equity (200 / (450+200)). J4o is a weak, poorly-playing hand out of position that is heavily dominated by a button raiser’s range (lots of Jx, Ax/Kx, suited broadways, connectors and pairs). It has minimal postflop playability and few clean ways to realize equity OOP. A light 3-bet is possible in some dynamics, but with deep stacks and no strong blockers I’m not comfortable creating that spot vs this opponent. Folding preserves a deep stack for better spots and avoids marginal postflop situations. I will fold.

3-handed on the button with T2s suited connectors. Wide opening range appropriate here as first to act preflop. Hand has straight and flush potential, good playability postflop. My stack is 46BB, need to build it; folding too tight, limping passive. Opponents deep-stacked and aggressive from history, but position allows control. Standard open to 3x BB (300).

I’m in the small blind (posted 50) facing a button open to 300 (pot 450). My hand Q♠4♥ is a weak offsuit holding that plays very poorly out of position, especially multiway if the big blind defends. Calling 250 requires ~35.7% equity (250 / (450+250)) to break even — Q4o rarely has that vs a button open range and will be dominated a large share of the time. A light 3-bet to 500 is unattractive: it has poor blockers, risks creating a bigger pot OOP, and the deep BB is likely to defend or reraise; the short-stacked button could also shove, putting me in a tough spot with a weak hand. With a healthy stack I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold here.

I am in the Big Blind with a strong suited broadway hand (Qc Jc). Elon has raised from the Button to 300, which is a standard open. With 200 more to call into a pot of 450, I am getting excellent pot odds. While 3-betting is a strong alternative to take initiative, calling allows me to realize my hand's equity post-flop without over-committing against Elon's 43bb stack, which could potentially shove over a 3-bet.

I have missed this flop, and the Ace on board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. Being out of position against Elon, who has a relatively short stack, I will check to see how he proceeds. I have very limited equity, mainly consisting of backdoor club draws and potentially hitting a runner-runner straight, so I am looking to play cautiously.

Preflop raise from button with suited T2d was standard wide range play. Flop Ah 9c 7d completely misses me - no pair, but gutshot straight draw to 8 (78 9 T), and backdoor diamond flush draw. Flash checks as BB defender, representing weakness. As preflop aggressor in position, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet to fold out his weak Ax, draws, or worse pairs. Board has Ace, which hits some calling ranges but Flash's check suggests he doesn't have it. Pot 650, betting ~1/2 pot (325) applies pressure while keeping SPR manageable postflop if called (my stack 4300, pot would be ~1300, SPR ~3). Folding equity high here, and I can fold to raise.

I have missed the flop entirely with Qc Jc on an Ace-high board (Ah 7d 9c). The pre-flop raiser, Elon, has a significant range advantage on this board, as he is likely to hold all strong Aces, while my range is capped by only calling from the Big Blind. While I have a backdoor club draw, I have very little immediate equity and no direct straight draws. Given Elon's short stack of roughly 40bb, a 'float' or a semi-bluff is unattractive because he is likely to be committed if he has any piece of the board or an Ace. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play here.

Button with 7♥8♠ (offsuit) in a 3-handed pot is a clear open — good postflop playability and lots of fold equity. Pot is 150, to call 100; I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind) to take the initiative, apply pressure to the small blind and isolate the shorter big blind (Elon ~48–50 BB). This sizing is standard on this table, builds the pot when I have positional advantage, and keeps weaker blind defenses guessing. If I face a large 3‑bet shove I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold), but if called I’ll use position to c-bet favourable flops and extract value.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 4d, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Given the low strength of my cards and being out of position for the rest of the hand, there is no reason to defend this. Folding is the most prudent action.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 from Minni after SB Flash folds. Pot is 450, need to call 200 more (total 300 committed). 6d7d is a suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential. BB defense vs button should be wide due to position postflop (acting first but good odds: ~2.25:1). Button range is wide (suited broadways, Ax, connectors, etc.), and hand realizes well multiway or heads-up. Implied odds solid with my 48BB stack and opponents deep. Folding too tight; 3-betting not ideal as it's speculative without fold equity priority. Call to see flop cheap and play postflop.

Flop is As 3c 2s, a dry ace-high board with a spade flush draw possibility. My 67dd completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely with A blocker), no flush draw (diamonds), just backdoor potential. OOP as BB, pot 650, facing button range that's wide but hits ace often (Ax, KQs, etc.). Check-fold is optimal; no showdown value, low fold equity if I bet/raise, and speculative hand didn't connect. Preserve stack for better spots.

I was the preflop aggressor in a 3‑handed pot and Elon checked the flop — this board (3♣ A♠ 2♠) is great for a c‑bet: it contains an overcard (A) that hits my perceived range but misses a lot of calling ranges (broadways, medium pairs, weak Ax combos), and Elon’s check shows either weakness or a slow‑played Ace. My hand 7♥8♠ has little showdown value but has a single spade (remote backdoor equity) and excellent bluff equity as part of my c‑betting range. I want to apply pressure, fold out non‑Ace hands and small pairs, and keep pot control vs a caller. I’ll make a standard small/medium c‑bet of 300 into a 650 pot (~45% pot). If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn — give up to significant aggression (check/fold) on heavy resistance unless I pick up meaningful equity (paired board, spade turn, or clear read Elon is bluffing). If Elon check‑raises big I’ll fold — I don’t have the equity to continue.

On this As3c2s flop, my 67dd has completely missed: no pair, no straight draw (67 doesn't connect with 2-3-A), no flush draw (diamonds vs spade-heavy board). Pure backdoor equity at best. OOP in BB facing Minni's button continuation bet of 300 into ~650 (now pot 950), her wide button opening range hits this ace-high dry board frequently with Ax, broadway, or draws. Bet sizing is small (~1/2 pot), but check-fold is standard as I have zero showdown value, minimal fold equity if raising, and no implied odds justify calling (stack 4650 ~46BB, but air OOP). Opponent history shows Minni bets flops aggressively post her opens. Preserve stack for premium spots; folding is +EV.

I am on the Button with 7-5 offsuit. This hand is significantly below the standard opening range for a three-handed game. Given that Elon is playing a shorter stack and Minni is quite aggressive, there is no reason to attempt a steal with such a weak hand. Folding is the most profitable decision in the long run.

Preflop in the small blind facing a fold from the button (Flash). My hand is K4o, which is offsuit and relatively weak. It plays poorly postflop, often dominated by better kings, and has limited playability against the big blind's wide defending range. With a short stack of 4600 relative to opponents (Flash 16900, Minni 18350), folding preserves chips for better opportunities. No need to invest the additional 50 into a marginal spot.

27o is one of the worst possible starting hands with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Even in a 3-handed spot on the button facing only the big blind after the SB likely folded (explaining pot of 150 dead money and to-call 100), this hand has terrible equity against the BB's calling or 3-bet range, poor post-flop playability, and isn't worth risking chips in a limped or calling spot. Standard fold to preserve stack for better opportunities. Steal with better holdings next time.

Heads-up from the small blind with 2♦ T♥. Pot is 150 and it’s only the BB to act — this hand is marginal, but in heads-up I should be opening a wide range and taking the initiative rather than completing and playing OOP. I’ll raise to 300 (a standard open size at this table) to generate fold equity and deny the BB good odds to continue. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet with this holding, call smaller defences and C-bet favorable flops, and play cautiously on highly coordinated or ace-heavy boards. Raising here maximizes long-term EV by forcing decisions and keeping pressure on Flash.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit (A3o). Minni, in the Small Blind, has raised to 300. This is a standard blind-vs-blind situation. A3o is ahead of a typical wide small-blind opening range but doesn't play exceptionally well post-flop. Given that I have a healthy stack and it's only 200 more to call into a pot of 400, I have the necessary pot odds to see a flop. Folding would be too tight, and while a 3-bet is an option to try and take it down now, calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate based on the flop texture.

I raised preflop and arrived with initiative heads-up. The flop Qd 9s 3c is a dry, rainbow board that misses a lot of cold-calling ranges. My 2♦T♥ has no pair and no real draw, but this is a textbook continuation-bet spot to charge unpaired overcards, small pairs and speculative hands that called preflop. I’ll lead for about half the pot to apply pressure but not commit too much with air — 300 into the 600 pot. If Flash folds I take it down; if he calls I proceed cautiously and look to improve (pairing the Ten) before investing further; if he check-raises or shows heavy turn aggression I will fold.

On the flop of 3c-Qd-9s, I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. Minni leads out with a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. This is a standard small-blind-vs-big-blind flop where the range of the small blind is quite wide. My bottom pair is sufficient to call at least one street, especially given the pot odds of 3-to-1 (300 to win 900). Raising would likely only get called by better hands (Queens, Nines, or strong draws), so calling is the most profitable play to keep her wider range of bluffs or weaker hands in the pot and see how the turn develops.

I opened from the SB and c-bet the dry Q-9-3 flop and was called — Flash’s calling range is weighted to Qx/9x/pairs and some broadways or two-card diamonds. The turn J♦ pairs the board and brings a second diamond, and my T♥2♦ is still unpaired. I have non-nut river outs (K and 8 for straights plus three remaining Tens) ≈ 11 outs -> ~24% raw, but many are “dirty” outs (can give villain better two‑pair/straights or complete his diamond-based draws). With the pot at 1,200 and both stacks deep relative to the pot, I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with air and face a check-raise or large turn bet that I’ll have to fold to. Pot-control is the highest EV line: check and re-evaluate on the river — call a reasonable turn stab to try to realize equity, fold to heavy aggression, and if checked to me again I’ll take my free river to bluff or value-bet a paired Ten.

On the turn, the Jack of Diamonds is a card that connects well with many hands in a Small Blind's opening range (like QJ, J9, JT, or T8). However, Minni has checked, which often indicates either a hand that wants to see a cheap showdown (like a weak 9 or a Jack) or a complete air hand that has given up. My bottom pair of threes with an Ace kicker has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value, nor is it a particularly effective bluffing candidate. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and can evaluate the river based on her next action. Betting would likely only fold out hands I already beat and get called or raised by hands that have me beat.

I have T♥2♦ on a 3c Qd 9s Jd 9c runout — I missed everything and have no showdown value. Flash called my flop c-bet and then checked the turn; that line is weighted to medium-strength hands (Qx, Jx, 9x, some pairs) and sticky callers rather than pure float/bluff hands. To profitably bluff the 1200 pot I’d need Villain to fold roughly b/(P+b) — e.g. a 400 bet needs >25% fold frequency — and given his flop-call/turn-check profile I expect a call frequency higher than that. With both stacks deep a failed bluff or a call would be costly, and I have essentially no value hands to target. Pot control and preservation of stack equity is the highest EV line: check to showdown and fold to any river raise.

On the river, the board is Qd-Jd-9s-9c-3c. I hold Ad-3s, which gives me two pair: Nines and Threes with an Ace kicker. Minni has checked both the turn and the river after leading the flop. Her check on the river suggests she likely has a hand with limited showdown value or has given up on a bluff. My two pair (Nines and Threes) beats all of her missed draws (like 87, K8, etc.) and all Ace-high hands (which only have a pair of Nines with high kickers from the board). However, I lose to any Queen, any Jack, any Nine, or any pocket pair higher than 33 (44-88). Since I am unlikely to get called by a worse hand if I bet, and I'm unlikely to fold out a better hand (like a Jack or a small pair), checking back to realize my showdown value is the most prudent and standard play here.

Open-raise to 300 (3x) from the button with J♦8♠. In three-handed play my range should be wide — raising for initiative and position is better than limping or just calling 100. A 3x open gives good fold equity against the small blind and isolates the shorter-stacked big blind (Elon, ~45bb). If Elon jams over my raise I’ll fold this marginal hand; if I get callers I’ll use position to c-bet on favorable/dry flops and play pot-control on connected or dangerous boards.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c Tc, a strong suited connector. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Elon, in the Big Blind, is short-stacked with only 4500. While my hand plays well post-flop, raising here puts pressure on the Button and potentially isolates against the short stack, or simply takes down a decent pot immediately. However, calling is also viable to see a flop. Given the stacks and the hand strength, a 3-bet to 1000 is a standard aggressive play to take control of the hand.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button open-raise to 300 followed by a small blind 3-bet to 1000. My hand is 2d Th, which is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. My stack is 4500 (~45BB), shallow but not desperate. Calling 900 into a 1400 pot offers terrible pot odds (~28% equity needed, but this hand has near 0% vs typical 3-bet ranges which include pairs, Ax, broadways). Shoving ~4.5x pot risks my stack with no fold equity against strong ranges. Folding is mandatory to preserve stack for better spots. Opponents have deep stacks (17k+), but no reason to speculate here.

Flash 3-bet to 1,000 from the SB over my 300 button open. Jd8s is marginal offsuit but has playable equity and position post‑flop. With deep stacks and the pot ~1,400, calling the extra 700 (total 1,000) gives decent pot odds (~3:1) and lets me leverage position to realize equity. I don’t want to 4‑bet bluff here — too risky with this hand and Flash’s likely range — and folding is too nitty given my open and the cheap price. Plan: call, see the flop; continue on dry/favorable boards (value/double‑barrel when appropriate), play cautiously on ace/king heavy or coordinated boards, and fold to large turn/river aggression or a shove from Flash.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet. The flop is fairly dry with a Queen high (2s Qc 4d). This board generally favors the range of a 3-bettor, as I can have AA, KK, QQ, and AQ in my range. Although I only have 10-high with a backdoor flush draw, a standard continuation bet of 1/3 pot (700) is effective here to put pressure on Minni's non-Queen hands like pocket pairs (55-JJ) or Ace-high hands that missed. It's a high-frequency C-bet spot to take down the pot immediately.

I opened preflop and called Flash’s 3‑bet to keep initiative and use position. Flop Q♣ 4♦ 2♠ is very dry and a textbook spot to continuation‑bet — I likely c‑bet and Flash check‑raised to 700. Pot is 2,800; calling 700 gives me ~20% break‑even equity (700/(2800+700)=20%). J♦8♠ has ~6 clean outs to pair (3 jacks + 3 eights) — roughly a 24% chance to hit by river — and I also get to use position to make better decisions on the turn. Flash’s check‑raise is weighted toward Qx/sets and some bluffs, but raising as a bluff here with J8 is too thin and folding would be overly nitty given the cheap price. Plan: call the 700 to see the turn, fold to big turn/river aggression unless I improve (pair a J/8 or pick up a strong draw), and re‑evaluate for value/bluff opportunities when I hit or see a favorable runout.

The turn King is a good card for my perceived 3-betting range (AA, KK, AK), but I have absolute air (Ten-high) with no real draw to improve. Minni called both the pre-flop 3-bet and the flop continuation bet, which indicates she likely has a piece of the board, such as a Queen or a pocket pair. While the King is a scare card, firing a second barrel into a range that has already shown resistance is risky when I have zero equity. Checking here is the most prudent move; I can evaluate her response, but I am prepared to fold to a bet as I have no showdown value and very few outs.

Turn card (K♠) doesn't improve J♦8♠ — I have no pair and no real draw (only 6 outs to a pair, ~12–13% on the river). Flash's line (3‑bet pre, check‑raise flop) is weighted to strong value hands (Qx/sets/overpairs) though a bluff is possible; his check on the turn is ambiguous (trap or pot‑control). With a dry board, deep stacks and a 3‑bet/CR line behind me, I prefer pot control and preserving stack rather than firing a thin bluff that can be raised off. I’ll check and fold to meaningful turn/river aggression; if he checks back I can re‑evaluate the river and consider a delayed bluff only on a very favorable card.

I have Ten-high and no showdown value. After 3-betting pre-flop and continuation betting the flop, I checked the turn when the King arrived, which likely signaled to Minni that I was giving up or pot-controlling. Minni checked back on the turn, suggesting she has some showdown value like a Queen or a mid-pair and is happy to go to a cheap showdown. Since I have almost no chance of winning the hand if it goes to a check-down, the only way to win is to bluff. However, given her flop call and the way the board run out, it is unlikely she will fold a Queen or even a pocket pair to a single river bet. Checking is the most disciplined move here to minimize further losses.

I check. I hold J♦8♠ with no pair on 2♠–Q♣–4♦–K♠–7♠. Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop check‑raise) is weighted to strong value hands (Qx, sets, strong overpairs) or very well-timed bluffs — but on this river I have almost no showdown value and very little fold equity. The turn and river brought spade‑heavy cards (three spades on board); my single spade (8♠) does not make a flush, so there are plausible made hands in his range that beat me. Pot control and stack preservation are the priority — a thin river bluff risks a big raise and a large chunk of my 16,200 stack for very little expected gain in a spot where the opponent’s range is polarized toward value. I will check and fold to meaningful river aggression, and only continue if he shows clear weakness (which he hasn’t).

I am on the button in a three-handed game with Ace-Five offsuit. This is a standard opening hand to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. Elon in the small blind has a shorter stack (approx. 44 BBs), so I need to be mindful of a potential shove, but A5o is strong enough to open. I'll make a standard 3x raise to 300.

T2o is one of the weakest hands in poker, completely uncoordinated offsuit trash with minimal playability. Facing a button raise to 3x from Flash, who raises frequently from late position based on history, calling 250 OOP from SB offers terrible equity realization postflop. No straight or flush potential, dominated often, and reverse position against a wide range. Pot odds are insufficient (calling 250 into 450 for ~36% equity needed, but hand has <30% vs typical button range). Raising or shoving is suicidal with 44.5BB stack. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in 150-hand game.

I’m in the big blind with J♠6♦ and have already posted 100. Flash opened to 300, so I must put in 200 more (total 300) to see a flop. J6o is a marginal, often-dominated hand and I’m out of position vs the button, but the additional cost (200 into a 450 pot → I need ~31% equity to break even) plus deep stacks and frequent 3x button opens here justify defending my BB range. I’ll call and keep the pot manageable — plan to check and fold to large C-bets on dry A/K/Q flops unless I pair the J or pick up strong draws; proceed for value or larger bluffs only with clear equity. I won’t 3-bet light with this particular hand.

I have J♠6♦ on a K♠2♠7♣ flop and missed — no pair and only a runner‑runner spade backdoor. Villain was the BTN 3x preflop opener, so his C‑bet range contains a lot of kings/top pairs and many small continuation bluffs; I don’t want to build a pot out of position with essentially air. I’ll check to control the pot and to realize my showdown equity; I’ll fold to a standard/large C‑bet and only continue vs an unusually tiny bet (or if I pair the J or pick up a usable draw on the turn). Checking also keeps bluffs and turn‑plays available if villain checks back.

I have the range advantage on this King-high flop after being the pre-flop raiser. The board of Ks-7c-2s is relatively dry, and while I only have Ace-high with a backdoor nut flush draw, a continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on Minni's middle-to-low range. I will bet approximately half the pot to capitalize on my positional advantage and fold out her weaker holdings.

I checked the flop with J♠6♦ and Flash continuation‑bet 325 into the ~650 pot (pot is 975 now). Calling 325 would commit me to a final pot of ~1300, so I need roughly 25% equity to break even. I have no pair and only 6 clean one‑card outs (3 Jacks + 3 Sixes) — ~24% to make a pair by the river — plus a tiny runner‑runner spade chance. Many of those completed hands will still be behind typical BTN c‑bet/value hands (Kx, Ax, better pairs). Given I’m out of position, lack fold equity and meaningful draws, and the bet is a standard c‑bet size, my realized equity vs Flash’s likely range is well under the required 25%. Folding preserves a healthy stack and avoids playing a bloated pot with air, so I’ll fold.

Preflop on the button in a 3-handed pot with blinds posted (pot 150, to call 100). Holding 5h3d, a weak offsuit, gapped hand with poor playability, low equity, and no strong blockers or potential. Standard to fold trash from this spot to avoid tough postflop decisions despite positional advantage, preserving stack for better opportunities. Opponents are deep-stacked and have shown aggression historically, but no need to enter with marginal hand first in.

I have QhQs — a very strong pocket pair. With only the big blind to act and the pot already 150 (I’ve posted 50, need 50 to call), the correct play is to raise for value and to isolate the BB. Table tendencies show frequent 3bb opens, so I’ll size to 3x the big blind (300 total) to charge draws and weaker hands, build the pot, and keep post‑flop maneuverability. If Flash 3‑bets I’ll evaluate sizing and range — call smaller 3‑bets to play postflop or 4‑bet for value against heavy aggression — but the open here should be standard and aggressive.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 2:1 odds. Additionally, I will have position on Minni for the rest of the hand. Given the pot odds and the fact that I'm in position, defending with a hand like Jack-Nine offsuit is standard against a Small Blind opening range.

I flopped trips — QhQs with Qc on Jd Qc 2s — which is essentially the best hand right now. Flash (BB) called my 3x pre so his range is wide (Jx, KQ/AQ, mid pairs, suited connectors), and the flop is relatively dry (no flush draw, limited straight draws). I want to extract value and charge the few draws while keeping worse pairs and Jx hands in, so I’ll make a value bet of about half the pot. Betting 300 into 600 is large enough to get value from top pairs and medium pockets but small enough to induce calls from weaker holdings; if he raises I’ll re-evaluate (likely call or 4‑bet for value given stack sizes), if he calls I’ll continue extracting on safe turns.

On the flop of Jd-Qc-2s, I have second pair (Jacks) with a Nine kicker. Minni, the Small Blind, led out for 300 into a 600 pot (half-pot). While my Jack is a solid pair, the Queen is an overcard and Minni's range contains many Queens (AQ, KQ, QJ, QTs). However, Minni can also be betting with draws (KT, T9, flush draws aren't present) or weaker hands. Folding second pair in position at this stage would be too tight, and raising doesn't accomplish much since it likely only gets called by better hands and folds out worse. Calling allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate the turn while in position.

I hold QhQs — a set of queens on Jd Qc 2s 9h. Flop lead was called, so Flash’s range is wide (Jx, KQ/AQ, mid pairs, some single-card draws). The 9h on the turn is a relatively safe card and doesn’t bring many new hands that beat me. My objective is to extract value from one-pair hands (Jx, 9x), KQ/AQ and medium pairs while charging any backdoor draws, without pricing those hands out. Betting ≈1/2 pot keeps worse hands and pairs in and builds the pot. I’ll bet 600 into the 1200 pot. If Flash check-raises big, I’ll re-evaluate (likely call to get to river and extract more or shove for value vs a wide aggressive line depending on sizing).

On the turn, I have improved to two pair (Jacks and Nines). Minni's small bet of 600 into a 1200 pot (1/2 pot, making the total 1800) often indicates a marginal made hand like a Queen or a draw that wants to see a cheap river. While King-Ten has completed a straight, my two pair is extremely strong in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise here to extract value from her Queens (AQ, KQ, Q10) and various draws, and to protect my hand against overcards or straight-completing cards on the river.

I have trip Queens (QhQs with Qc on Jd Qc 2s 9h). Flash’s big check‑raise to 1,800 is large and polarizing, but very few hands actually beat me — mainly rare 10‑8 straights — while his believable calling/raising range contains lots of Jx, KQ/AQ, 9x and medium pairs that I crush. The pot is 3,600 and calling 1,200 requires only ~25% equity, which my set comfortably has versus a realistic range. Raising to 3,000 would fold out a lot of the hands I want to extract value from and risks a big 3‑bet; calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in and lets me re‑evaluate the river. I’ll call the 1,200 and plan to extract more on the river or call a shove unless the board brings a clear straight/full‑house scare.

I have QhQs — trips on Jd Qc 2s 9h 3s. Flash’s line (calling pre and the flop then a big turn check‑raise) is polarizing: it can contain Jx, KQ/AQ, 9x and medium pairs as well as the rare 10‑8 straight that actually beats me. On the river the board is benign — the only realistic hands that beat me are 10‑8 straights (very few combos). I am therefore far ahead of most of his range and should extract thin value rather than shut out callers or concede showdown value. A modest value bet (~1/3 pot) will get called by Jx, 9x, KQ/AQ and other worse hands while avoiding folding them out or creating a bloated pot where I could be dominated by the rare straight. I’ll make a small value bet of 1,600 to maximize calls from worse hands and protect my equity against the small number of hands that beat me.

On the river, the board is Jd-Qc-2s-9h-3s. I have two pair, Jacks and Nines. Minni led out on the river for 1600 into a pot of 4800. My turn raise was called, and Minni leading into me again on the river usually signifies a strong hand or a block-bet with a hand like a Queen. The only hand that beat me on the turn was KT for a straight or a set of Queens/Jacks (unlikely given the turn call). The Trey of Spades is a blank. My two pair is still very high in my range for this line. Raising might isolate me against KT or a set, while calling ensures I win against any Queen or missed draw Minni might be turning into a small lead-bet. Given the pot odds (1600 to win 6400), a call is mandatory and the most prudent value-capturing move.

K♥Q♥ on the button is a very strong opening hand — great blockers and plenty of postflop playability in position. The table standard is a 3x open (many players open to 300 here), so I want to seize the initiative, build the pot when I'm in position, and fold out weaker blind holdings. With stacks: I'm very deep (234BB), SB (Flash) is deep (121BB) and BB (Elon) is around 43BB — a 300 open gives fold equity but keeps the pot manageable if I face calls. Plan: if called I'll play postflop in position (frequent C-bets on favorable textures, pot-control on dry boards); if I face a medium 3-bet I will often call and play postflop, but respect large 3-bets/all-ins depending on sizing and ranges. Optimal preflop is a standard raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with T2o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and the tendency of opponents to be aggressive, there is no reason to defend this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

3s4h is a complete trash hand with no straight or flush potential worth playing. Minni's button raise is likely wide, but calling 200 into 650 pot SPR commits too much with this hand that rarely improves enough to win. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with K8o. This is a standard late-position opening hand in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack and want to put pressure on the blinds. Opening for 3x (300 total) has been my standard size, and it remains effective here.

Pocket sevens is a strong premium hand preflop, well above the calling range and deserving of a 3-bet for value against a button raise, which is likely wide given Flash's aggressive history from previous hands. 3-betting builds the pot, gains dead money from the blinds, protects against overcards, and applies pressure. Calling OOP risks difficult postflop spots with a short-to-medium stack (43BB), while folding is too tight. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is standard and balanced. Minni in BB may fold or call, but we have fold equity.

Situation: Button (Flash) opened to 300 and the small blind (Elon) 3‑bet to 900. Pot is 1,300 and it costs me 800 to call (min‑raise to 1,500). I hold J♦4♦ in the big blind.

Equity/pot math: If I call 800 the pot will be 2,100, so I need ~38% equity to make a pure call profitable. J4♦ is a weak, highly dominated suited hand versus a typical SB 3‑bet range (pockets, A/K broadways, suited Aces) and my realistic equity is well under that number — and it only gets worse if Flash defends/calls behind (multiway).

Opponent/read factors: Elon’s 3‑bet size and remaining shorter stack make this look like a value/isolation 3‑bet or a polarized shove‑range; Flash behind can and likely will call or 4‑bet, creating difficult post‑flop spots out of position. I have a big stack but there’s no reason to force action with a marginal holding that plays poorly multiway and is often dominated.

Conclusion: Fold. Preserve stack for better spots with hands that have real equity and playability vs 3‑bets, or consider 4‑bet bluffs only with much stronger blockers/broadway combos.

I am on the button with K8o and have already put in 300 chips. Elon, who is a relatively short stack (approximately 34-35 big blinds total), has 3-bet me to 900 from the small blind. While I have position on Elon, K8o is a weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, even in a three-handed game. Elon's 3-betting range is likely to dominate me (containing better Kings, Aces, and pairs). Additionally, calling 600 more into a 1300 pot with a mediocre hand often leads to difficult post-flop decisions when facing a short-stack shove. It is more profitable to fold here and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my chip lead.

On the button with Ah5s, a suited ace with good playability and potential for strong hands like nut flush draws, straights, or top pair. Pot is 150 from blinds, to call 100 suggests likely folded to me (SB probably folded). This is a prime stealing spot against big stacks in blinds. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) applies pressure, builds pot with position, and takes down blinds frequently. My stack of 4750 (~47BB) can handle this; implied odds good if called. Calling is passive; folding too tight.

Elon (button) opened to 300. I’m in the small blind with Qh Ts (offsuit). QTo is marginal out of position but has decent broadway/straight potential and, with my deep stack (234bb), I have enough implied odds to see a flop. 3-betting OOP for thin value is suboptimal and folding is too passive given equity and position dynamics (button’s open range is likely wide). I’ll call the 250 to see a flop and then evaluate: continue aggressively with a pair or strong draw, fold or play very cautiously on dry boards against significant aggression, and fold to a sizable preflop 3-bet unless the size/reads justify otherwise.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce suited. Elon opened from the Button to 3bb, and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting good pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 900), Ten-Deuce is an extremely weak hand that is easily dominated, especially when playing out of position against two active opponents. The potential to win a large pot is limited by Elon's relatively short stack (around 45BB) and the poor playability of the hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold QhTs on A♠ 4♣ Q♦ — that’s second pair (queens) with a vulnerable T kicker and I’m out of position vs the button. Elon’s open range contains a lot of A-x and broadways, so the Ace on board significantly reduces the absolute strength of my hand. The flop is very dry (no real draws), so there’s little to be gained by barreling OOP; instead I want pot control and to realize my showdown equity. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets Elon bluff/c-bet with worse or marginal hands, and allows me to call small-to-medium c-bets for value. Plan: check now. If Elon bets a small-to-medium sizing (roughly 1/3–1/2 pot) I will call to extract value from worse queens/Kx/weak Ax and to go to a cheap turn. If he check-raises or fires a large/pot-sized bet I will give credit to his range (polarized to Ax/two-pair/sets) and fold most of the time. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the turn and either take a stab on a safe card or continue controlling the pot to get to showdown. With pot = 700 and his effective stack ~4450 (SPR ≈ 6.4), pot-control OOP with a vulnerable second pair is the correct, exploitative line.

Flop gives me top pair (aces) with a weak 5 kicker on a As4cQd rainbow board. Board is fairly dry with no strong draws, possible gutshots or weak Qx hands. Minni called preflop from SB and checked, representing weakness. Position on button is excellent for controlling the pot. Betting for value and protection against draws/improvements. Pot 700, bet ~55% pot (400) balances value from worse aces/pairs while protecting against Qx or floats. My stack ~44BB effective against her deep stack, but sizing manageable. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing bluffs.

Board A♠ 4♣ Q♦, I have QhTs — second pair with a vulnerable T-kicker and I'm out of position. I checked the flop to control the pot and allow Elon to barrel with worse Qx or bluffs. Elon bets 400 into ~700 (pot now 1100) — a small-to-medium c-bet from a button open that can include a lot of Ax, broadways and bluffs. Raising OOP with a single pair and a weak kicker would be reckless (inflates the pot and faces many better hands); folding is too weak because I have real showdown value vs worse queens and some wandering broadways. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity and lets me re-evaluate on the turn. After my call the SPR will be low (~2.4), so I can fold to large turn size-ups if he shows heavy aggression, but will continue vs checks or small turn bets. Call 400 to see the turn.

I have Q♥ T♠ — second pair with a weak kicker, out of position. I checked the flop to control the pot and Elon fired a small c-bet that I called. The turn (7♠) is a relatively blank and Elon checked it back, which narrows his range toward both missed hands and showdown/Ax type hands he doesn’t want to inflate. This is exactly the spot to keep the pot small and realize showdown value rather than barreling OOP with a vulnerable pair. Betting here would risk getting check‑raised by stronger hands (A, two‑pair/sets) or bloating the pot when I’m behind, and it will fold out some worse hands I’d happily see a river against. I’ll check and evaluate the river: call small river bets for value versus worse queens/KQ, fold to large polarized overbets, and consider a thin bluff only if the river texture and read justify it.

Turn 7s completes my nut flush draw (As,7s,5s) while keeping top pair aces (weak 5 kicker). Combined, this is an extremely strong hand with ~60%+ equity against most calling ranges. Board has possible Qx, weaker Ax, 77/44 sets, but my draw is nutted and pairs the board weakly. Minni checked flop (after calling 400 into 1100ish) and turn again, showing weakness—likely Qx, mid pairs, missed draws, or floats. Position allows pot control, but betting extracts value from worse pairs/draws and protects against river villains. Pot 1500, effective stack 4050 (~27 pot-sized bets). Half-pot bet (750) is standard but sizing up slightly to 900 pressures her deep stack, balances as value since she check-calls flops often (per history: Hand 14,19,44, etc.). Checking risks free river card to her improving (e.g., Ks for str8? unlikely, or pair). Max EV to bet.

Elon (button) has opened preflop and continued on a very dry A♠-4♣-Q♦-7♠ board, betting 900 into a 2400 pot. I have Q♥T♠ — top pair but a very weak kicker and I’m out of position. Call requires ~27% equity (900 into a 3300 pot after calling), but his line (open -> c-bet -> sizable turn bet) is heavily weighted toward value (A‑x, two‑pair/set, and better Qx with superior kickers). There are almost no believable bluffs on this runout, so my realistic equity is well below the threshold. Raising OOP with a marginal second pair would be reckless and would often commit me when behind. With a deep stack and plenty of future spots, folding and preserving chips is the best exploitative play here.

I'm on the button with 3♣7♥ — a weak, unsuited, non-connecter. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop (min-raise to 200). Calling 100 into 150 gives me only ~2.5:1 on the pot (I need ≈40% equity to breakeven), which this hand does not have multiway against two opponents. Raising to try to steal is overly thin here — SB (Flash) often defends and BB (Elon) has shown frequent preflop aggression and a willingness to 3-bet or shove with his shorter stack. With a deep stack and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better, playable hand is the higher EV play. Fold.

I am in the Small Blind with AJo, a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. The Button (Minni) has folded. I am facing the Big Blind (Elon), who has a relatively short stack of 5,450 compared to my 11,650 and the starting stacks. Since it is folded to me, I want to raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and build the pot with a premium hand. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to either take the blinds or play a pot in position or with the initiative.

AKo is one of the best hands in poker, especially suited? No, offsuit, but still premium. Flash raised from SB after Button fold, so likely a wide stealing range including many weaker aces, broadways, suited connectors. As BB, I have position postflop. Calling is fine but 3-betting isolates, applies pressure, and builds the pot with a strong hand that plays well postflop. Standard 3-bet size over a 300 raise is to 900. My stack of 5450 can handle it, pot odds are irrelevant since it's a value raise. Folding is never correct.

I hold AJo in the Small Blind. After my initial raise, Elon in the Big Blind has 3-bet to 900. AJo is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game, especially in a blind-vs-blind situation. Elon is relatively short-stacked with about 4,650 remaining. Folding is too conservative here given the strength of my hand. While 4-betting is an option to put maximum pressure on his stack, calling allows me to see a flop and keep his wider 3-betting range in the pot. I will call the additional 600 to see the flop.

On this flop of Jc-Kh-3d, I have middle pair with an Ace kicker. The pot is 1800 and my opponent, Elon, has a remaining stack of 4650. Since Elon was the pre-flop 3-bettor, this board texture (containing a King and a Jack) is generally favorable for his range, but I have a solid piece of it. Checking here is the standard play as the caller of a 3-bet, allowing me to see how he reacts. If he bets, I have a very easy call given the strength of my hand and the potential to improve to two pair or trips, or even just win if he's continuation betting with hands like AQ or smaller pairs.

We are heads-up on the flop with AcKs, holding top pair top kicker (kings with ace kicker) on a dry board of Jc Kh 3d. The pot is 1800 after preflop 3-bet and call. Flash checked from SB, which is standard but could represent a wide range including weaker pairs, draws, or floats given they called the 3-bet preflop. Possible villain hands: KQ, KJ, QJ, AJ, ATs, sets (unlikely), or bluffs. This board misses most of Flash's calling range but hits some. Betting for value and thin value against weaker Kx, Ax, Jx, and to protect against gutshots (QT, 43s, etc.) or overcards. Half-pot or 2/3 pot bet sizes work well here; 1200 (2/3 pot) builds the pot while denying equity to draws and floats. Checking risks giving free cards to improve hands or bluffs. My stack (4650) and villain's (10800) support this; SPR remains manageable post-bet.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a J-K-3 rainbow board. While the King is certainly in Elon's 3-betting range, I cannot fold such a strong hand in a 3-handed game to a single continuation bet. Calling is the best option to keep his potential bluffs like AQ in the hand and to see how the turn develops. Since Elon has a relatively short stack remaining (3450), I am prepared to go to showdown depending on the turn card.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with an Ace kicker on a board of J-K-3-3. The board pairing on the turn with a 3 is generally a blank card that shouldn't have improved Elon's 3-betting range. I am first to act on the turn. Since Elon has been the aggressor throughout the hand and the SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) is very low (0.8), checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to see how he chooses to proceed with his remaining chips; leading into him would only get called by hands that have me beat (like Kings or better) and might force him to fold bluffs that I want him to continue with.

Turn board pairs the innocuous 3, bringing a possible heart flush draw but primarily a blank for most villain ranges. Holding TPTK (AcKs on KhJc3d3h), which remains ahead of villain's likely check-call range from SB: weaker Kx (KQ, KJ), Jx, QQ/TT/99 (overpairs vulnerable now), AJ/AQ floats, or stubborn draws. Sets (JJ) possible but narrow; 33 crushed preflop. Villain's turn check after flop check-call suggests not super strong (might bet trips/sets), possibly weakness or pot control. With pot at 4200 and my stack 3450 (villain 9600), SPR ~1.7: betting commits most of stack but denies equity to any live draws (hearts ~9 outs, gutshots crushed), extracts value from worse, and pressures floats/overpairs fearing check-raise/shove. Checking risks free river cards improving villain (river A/J/K helps some foes, hearts bad) or induces unnecessary bluffs while pot grows uncontrollably. Half-pot bet of 2100 builds pot, keeps SPR low, standard continuation sizing post-flop value bet/call. Superior to checking given range advantage and board texture.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with an Ace kicker on a board of J-K-3-3. After calling a 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop, the turn card (a 3) is a relative blank. Elon is continuing his aggression with a bet of 2100 into a 4200 pot. With Elon having only 1350 chips left behind, he is essentially pot-committed. Given the pot odds (calling 2100 to win a total pot of 8400, or 25%) and the 3-handed nature of the game, folding a hand as strong as AJo is too tight. While he could easily have a King or an overpair, his range also includes bluffs like AQ, AT, or smaller pocket pairs that he is turning into a semi-bluff. I will call to see the river.

The river 6d is a complete brick that does not change the board state significantly. I have middle pair (Jacks) with an Ace kicker. Elon has been aggressive since pre-flop, and his remaining stack is very small (1,350) compared to the pot (8,400). By checking, I allow Elon to shove his remaining chips with any air or bluffs (like AQ or AT) that he might have been barreling. If I bet, I might force him to fold those few bluffs, whereas if I check, I am committed to calling his tiny remaining stack regardless of whether I think he has a King or AA. Checking is the standard play for a bluff-catcher in this spot.

On the river, holding AcKs for top pair top kicker (Kings and Ace kicker) on a board of Jc Kh 3d 3h 6d. The paired 3s and river 6d are relatively blank; no flush completes, and straight draws missed. Pot is 8400 with my stack at 1350 (SPR ~1:6), and villain (Flash) has checked after check-calling substantial flop (1200) and turn (2100) bets from a wide SB calling range vs my preflop 3-bet. Villain's range is capped: likely weaker Kx (KQ/KJ), Jx (AJ/QJ), overpairs (QQ/TT), or unlikely sets (JJ/33). My hand crushes most of this range and beats bluffs/floats. With tiny remaining stack, shoving 1350 extracts maximum value from worse hands villain may hero-call (e.g., KJ, JJ), denies any fold equity misstep by checking, and villain's 7500 stack can afford the call light. Checking back risks free showdown losses to better hands (rare) or missing value from worse. Standard play: bet all-in for thin value and protection.

I am holding middle pair (Jacks) with an Ace kicker on a board of J-K-3-3-6. The pot is currently 9,750 and I only need to call 1,350 to see the showdown. This gives me pot odds of approximately 7.2 to 1 (or needing to be right about 12.2% of the time). Given the 3-handed nature of the game and the fact that Elon's 3-betting range can include hands like AQ, ATs, or smaller pairs he turned into bluffs, I cannot fold such a strong bluff-catcher for such a small price relative to the pot. Even if he frequently shows up with a King, I only need to win a small fraction of the time to make this call profitable.

I am on the button with 6s4h. This is a very weak hand. While the button is the best position at the table, 64o is well outside of a standard opening range. Considering the stacks of my opponents and my own stack size of 6150 (approx 61 BBs), there is no reason to enter the pot with such a marginal hand when I will likely face resistance from Elon or Minni. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

A7o is a profitable hand to raise from the small blind against the big blind in this multiway but now heads-up spot after Flash folds. It has an ace blocker reducing the likelihood of facing AA/AK, solid high card strength, and good playability postflop. The pot odds to call are favorable but limping is too passive and invites multiway pots or raises. Raising builds the pot when ahead of Minni's wide defending range and applies fold equity. Minni has shown aggression but also folds to pressure in blinds frequently based on history. Sizing to 300 (3x BB) is standard for positionally advantaged steal attempt with deep stacks relative to raise size.

Elon opened to 300 from the small blind (button) — heads-up his opening range is wide, but he retains post‑flop position. J♦3♠ is a marginal hand, but defending the big blind here is correct: the extra 200 is cheap relative to the pot (pot 400, call 200 -> 300 total committed) and both stacks are deep so I have implied odds to realize equity. 3‑betting with J3o is unattractive because I’ll still be out of position postflop and the hand plays poorly in large pots. Plan: call to see the flop; if I miss I’ll check‑control and fold to heavy action, but if I pair the J or pick up a draw I’ll play aggressively for value.

Preflop raise with A7o from SB was standard for fold equity and position against BB defend. Flop is Kd5d2d, a king-high monotone diamond board, extremely wet and favorable to Minni's BB calling range (Kx, Adx, suited diamonds, pairs that connect). My hand is ace-high with only backdoor flush draw (one diamond) and no pair, zero equity against her value range and minimal fold equity for a c-bet OOP. Betting air here risks check-raise or call from better; checking allows pot control, realization of equity if she checks behind, and easy fold to her bet. Long-term EV positive to check-fold this spot given board texture and my range disadvantage postflop OOP.

Flop: 5♦ 2♦ K♦; I have J♦3♠. I picked up a very playable semi‑bluff — one diamond in hand plus three on board gives me a single‑card flush draw (9 outs) and I also have three jacks for pair outs (total ~12 outs). That’s roughly ~45% to improve to a pair/flush by the river and ~25% to hit on the turn. Elon opened wide from the SB heads‑up and then checked the flop, showing weakness or reluctance to build the pot. I should take the initiative with a semi‑bluff to get folds from weaker Kx and other non‑diamond hands while building the pot when called. Betting ~2/3 pot balances fold equity and allows me to realize equity if called. If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to extreme aggression; call only if the pot odds justify it).

Preflop raise with A7o was standard, but flop Kd5d2d is a nightmare: monotone diamond board favors BB defender's range heavily (Kx, diamond draws, suited connectors). As7d is ace-high no pair, backdoor diamond draw at best. Minni's donk bet of 400 into 600 screams strength (top pair Kx, flush draws like AdQd/Jd/Td, sets) over bluffs. Pot odds 400 to 1400 (~22% needed) but equity <15% vs her betting range; check-fold maximizes EV OOP by avoiding tough turn spots and preserving stack for better situations. History shows Minni aggressive but this board+action fits value.

Folded to me on the button preflop with 3c4c (suited connectors). This is a prime stealing spot against the blinds. 34s has excellent playability postflop, good implied odds if called, especially in position deep vs Minni and with Flash's 60BB stack. Opponents from history defend blinds somewhat loosely but fold frequently to button opens. Raising to 3x (300) balances steal equity with value/protection. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots OOP for blinds; better to take initiative. Folding too tight given position and hand potential.

I have 6♣6♠ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I need 250 more). This is a classic set-mining spot: pocket sixes play well calling here because stacks are deep and I want to preserve implied odds rather than bloating the pot OOP with a marginal 3-bet. A min 3-bet to 500 would give me little fold equity and still leave me out of position versus the button and the big blind. Calling keeps my range natural, gives me the chance to hit a set (≈11.8% on the flop) and extract maximum value when I do, and lets me fold to extreme postflop aggression if I miss. If the big blind folds and it’s heads-up, I’ll play the flop cautiously OOP and look to realize implied odds; if I flop a set I’ll get aggressive. Therefore I will call the raise (match the 300).

I am in the Big Blind with 2d 3d. Elon (Button) raised to 300, and Minni (Small Blind) called. While I am getting excellent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 700), 2-3 suited is one of the weakest hands in poker and realizes its equity very poorly, especially out of position against two opponents. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a stronger hand than to put chips in with a hand that is likely dominated or will be forced to fold on most flops.

I called preflop with 6♣6♠ intending to set-mine but missed — the flop Js Ad Ah completely bricks my plan. This board is very bad for a small pocket pair: any Ace in Elon’s raising range has trips, many strong hands and pairs (or at least an Ace or a Jack) beat me, and I have no meaningful backdoor draw. I’m out of position vs the preflop raiser, so I should exercise pot control and avoid getting bluffed/raised off the pot or inflated by a value hand. I will check and plan to fold to a continuation bet (only consider calling a very tiny probe or a clear blocking/river plan), but I will not lead or bluff-raise here with such a weak holding.

Preflop button raise with 3c4c was a standard steal attempt in position against loose blind defenses observed in history. Flop Js Ad Ah is a great spot for a continuation bet: paired aces board hits my perceived BTN range (Ax, broadways, some suited connectors) while capping villain's SB calling range somewhat. Minni's check suggests weakness (no strong Ax or better likely, as she'd lead or raise), providing fold equity. No showdown value with air, so betting extracts value from worse or takes it down. Position allows controlling pot if check-called. Sizing 400 (roughly 55% pot) balances value (weak Ax, Jx) and bluffs, denying equity to draws (spades, gutshots), while keeping stack committed low (~4% of 10.5k). Check risks giving free card to villain's floats/OESD, suboptimal long-term. Opponent history shows Minni folds to aggression postflop when missed.

Board is Js Ad Ah and I hold 6♣6♠. I checked the flop as planned — this board is extremely bad for a small pocket pair: any Ace in Elon’s range is trips and many Jx hands or pocket pairs also beat me. Elon’s 400 into a 1,100 pot is small but still gives me only ~26.7% pot odds (400/(1100+400)). I have no backdoor draws and very little equity vs a realistic c-bet range (many Ax, Jx, and value hands). Out of position, calling 400 just to see a turn lets Elon barrel me off further or get value when I’m behind. A bluff-raise OOP is too risky and unlikely to fold enough of his value range. With deep stacks but no implied odds here, the correct play is to preserve chips and fold.

A♠Q♠ on the Button in a three-handed pot is a strong, positionally advantaged hand — we should take the initiative. Open-raising isolates the blinds, gets value from worse Ax and broadway combos, and lets us play the flop in position. A standard 3xBB open to 300 is consistent with table norms (and well within our deep stack), applies pressure to the SB and BB, and keeps the pot manageable. If called we’ll C-bet appropriate sizing on favorable textures; if 3‑bet we’ll re-evaluate (can 4‑bet for value or call depending on opponent sizing and tendencies).

I am in the Small Blind with JdTh, a decent speculative hand. Minni (Button) has raised to 300. Minni is the table chip leader and has been quite active. Calling 250 more to see a flop with a hand that has good playability (straight potential, two overs to many low boards) is reasonable, though playing out of position against a big stack is difficult. However, folding here is a bit too tight given the pot odds and the hand strength. I will call to see a flop.

Preflop in BB with Ad2h facing a button raise to 300 and SB call. Pot 700, calling 200 for ~22% equity needed, but A2o is offsuit with poor playability OOP multiway. Ace blocker but weak kicker, easily dominated by better aces in opponents' calling ranges. Button likely wide, SB call speculative but still ahead often. Folding preserves stack for better spots in deepstack game.

I have Jack-high on a King-high board with two undercards and a backdoor straight draw. Minni raised pre-flop and the flop texture (K-7-6) is generally better for the pre-flop aggressor's range. I have no pair and no real draw. I will check and see what Minni does; if she bets, I will likely fold as I have very little equity.

We opened to 300 from the button and Flash (SB) just flat-called. Flop K♠ 7d 6c is a dry, high-card board that connects well with the SB’s defending range (lots of Kx and medium pairs), but Flash checked — showing either a missed holding or a desire to pot-control. Holding A♠Q♠ I have the best high-card (two overcards to the board) and some backdoor potential, but I do not have a made hand. In position I want to apply pressure and seize the initiative: a continuation bet will fold out many unpaired hands and weaker broadways, extract thin value from A‑x/Q‑x, and keep control of the pot. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (350) to balance fold equity with pot control — small enough not to commit me with just Ace‑high, big enough to get lots of folds from the SB’s wide parts of range. If Flash raises I’ll re-evaluate (most raises represent a king or strong pair and I’ll often fold Ace‑high), if he calls I’ll plan to barreled turns on favorable cards (A/Q/spade) or check‑behind to control the pot on dangerous turns.

I have Jack-high with no significant draws on a King-high board. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet. Given my lack of equity and the fact that I am out of position, I will fold and conserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 4d 3c, which is a very weak hand. Even in a 3-handed game, this hand lacks the high card value or connectivity to be played profitably against the blinds, particularly given that Minni has a large stack and is aggressive. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Preflop in SB heads-up vs BB with 96d suited connectors. Pot 150, to call 50 (3:1 odds). This hand has good playability postflop: suited, connected, potential for straights/flushes/draws. Folding is too tight from SB; raising OOP with a speculative hand risks playing a bloated pot without position advantage. Limping keeps pot small, realizes equity vs a loose BB like Minni who often calls/raises wide based on history. Postflop, we'll have initiative potential and implied odds with deep effective stacks (~11k). Best play: call.

Heads-up in the big blind with QcTs against a limp from the small blind. Elon’s limp indicates a wide, often weak range; with deep stacks and a playable hand like QTs I want to isolate and take the initiative. A standard raise to 300 total (raising 200 more over my 100 BB) prices out worse hands, gives fold equity, and keeps postflop pots manageable. If he folds I pick up the 200 pot; if he calls I can c-bet favorable flops or play positionally against his likely wide range. If he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or proceed cautiously).

Facing a raise to 300 from Minni in BB after my SB limp with 96dd suited connectors. Pot is 400, calling 200 more for 3:1 direct odds into a 600 pot, with deep stacks (my 11k effective). Minni's range is wide given history of frequent preflop raises and calling stations postflop. 96dd has excellent playability: flush potential, straight draws (78T), overcards possible. OOP but heads-up, implied odds high vs her loose tendencies. Folding too tight for pot odds and hand equity (~30-40% vs wide BB raise range). Raising risks bloated pot OOP without strong hand. Call to realize equity, control pot size, and exploit postflop.

Heads-up on flop as SB OOP with 9d6d vs Minni's BB raise range after my limp-call. Board Ah7s4c rainbow is dry but Ace-high, favoring her range which includes Ax, pairs, broadways from her frequent preflop raising. My hand has minimal equity: gutshot straight draw to 5 (about 4 outs), backdoor diamond flush draw, and live 9/6 overcards, but ~20-25% equity max vs her wide range, dropping lower vs Ax-heavy. No strong reason to bet: low fold equity on A-high board vs loose BB who calls wide postflop per history (e.g., Hands 17,18,19,47,54 etc.). Raising pointless with air. Pot 600 deep effective (~10k), checking realizes equity for cheap turn card, controls pot OOP, and induces bluffs/checks from missed draws in her range. Fold to significant bet. Checking maximizes EV long-term.

Heads-up vs Elon who cold‑called my preflop raise. Board A♠ 7♠ 4♣ (I hold Q♣ T♠) — I missed the flop (no pair, no realistic backdoor flush/straight). As the preflop aggressor I should c‑bet a meaningful but controlled size to fold out worse broadway hands and unpaired cards while avoiding bloating the pot if I’m up against A‑x or a set. Pot is 600, so I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (300). This gives fold equity, keeps lines manageable if called, and lets me re‑evaluate on the turn (check‑behind to pot‑control if I don’t improve or continue against obvious floats). If Elon check‑raises big I’ll be prepared to fold QTs; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn.

Facing a 300 bet into 900 pot (3:1 odds) OOP with 9d6d on Ah7s4c flop. Hand has no pair, no real draw (vague backdoor flush/straight potential, live 9), ~15-25% equity at best vs Minni's BB flop betting range which is Ax-heavy, pocket pairs, and some bluffs but favors value on dry Ace-high board after her preflop raise vs limp. Deep stacks but no implied odds with air/live cards OOP; calling builds pot OOP with marginal equity, sets up tough turn decisions. Raising has low fold equity vs loose history (frequent calls postflop). Pot odds insufficient long-term; folding preserves stack, avoids spew. Consistent with prior plan to fold to significant bet.

3-handed preflop on the button with pot 150 and to-call 100 (SB Minni folded her 50, BB Flash posted 100 and checked). This is a standard blind steal spot facing a checked BB. 6d7c offsuit is a playable hand in position with good playability (open-ended straight potential, two gutshots possible), fold equity against BB who likely has a wide checking range, and position for all future streets. Flash has 5650 (~57BB), deep enough to defend but vulnerable to button aggression. Standard play is to raise 2.5-3x the BB. Raising to 300 builds the pot, applies pressure, and denies equity to BB's marginal hands. Calling 100 limps weak OOP postflop; folding discards too much equity in position. History shows Flash defends sometimes but folds to aggression often (e.g., Hand 41, 55). With deep stacks and 150-hand goal, stealing blinds frequently maximizes EV.

I’m in the small blind with 5♦6♥ facing a button open to 300. This hand is too weak and too low-suited to play out of position. Calling 250 into a 450 pot would require ~36% equity to break even (250/(450+250)) and likely turns into a multiway OOP pot if the big blind acts — a poor spot for a low offsuit connector. A 3‑bet bluff to 500 has thin fold equity against a button opener and risks getting 4‑bet or called by the BB, putting me in difficult postflop spots with a weak hand. With my stack size I can wait for a better, positionally favorable spot. Fold and preserve the stack.

I am in the Big Blind with T3o. Elon, who has a healthy stack of 10,500, has raised to 300 from the button. Minni has folded. While it's only 200 more to call into a pot that will be 650, T3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Elon has been active and aggressive from the button. I don't see a reason to defend such a weak hand here given the stack depths and the disadvantageous position I would be in for the rest of the hand.

Button with Q♥9♦ — marginal but fine to open in position against only the blinds. Pot is 150 and the minimum raise is 200, but a standard 3x BB open to 300 gives me decent fold equity and isolates one or both blinds while keeping the pot manageable given opponent stack depths (Flash ~56bb, Elon ~108bb). Plan: take the initiative — if I get a single call I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops; if I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll fold this hand rather than bloating the pot out of position. Raise to 300 to steal/see a favorable flop in position.

I hold Ace-King of diamonds, a premium starting hand. Minni raised to 300 from the button, indicating a wide range. I will 3-bet to 1100 to build value and take control of the hand from the small blind. This sizing represents a strong range and forces the button to make a difficult decision with marginal hands while isolating the pot.

6d3c is one of the worst possible hands in the Big Blind, unsuited, low cards, disconnected. Facing a button open-raise to 300 from Minni and a strong 3-bet to 1100 from Flash in the SB, who has been aggressive in past hands. Pot is 1500, calling 1000 OOP gives poor odds (~1.5:1) with no equity realization potential postflop. Raising has zero fold equity with this trash and commits too much stack. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I opened to 300 on the button with Q♥9♦ and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1,100. To call is 800 more into a 1,500 pot (break‑even equity ≈ 800/2300 ≈ 34.8%). Flash has shown wide/aggressive preflop tendencies from the SB and his 3‑bet sizing is consistent with a mixed range, so Q9o has roughly the required equity and — crucially — I have positional advantage postflop. A 4‑bet bluff here is unnecessary and risky; folding surrenders too much equity and initiative given the price and position. I’ll call to realize equity and use position to navigate the flop: continue with a pair, good draw, or favorable texture; fold to concentrated aggression when unopened.

I hold Ace-King suited (diamonds) and I was the pre-flop 3-bettor. The flop comes Five-Jack-Ten with two spades. This board is quite coordinated and connects well with a button calling range (which likely includes hands like QJ, JT, T9, and various draws). Although I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen for a Broadway straight), I am out of position against a very active player (Minni) who has a massive stack. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot and see how Minni reacts. If she checks behind, I get a free turn card to potentially improve to a pair or my straight. If she bets, I can evaluate based on her sizing, as I still have significant equity with my overcards and gutshot.

Flash checked the flop. I’m on the button with Q♥9♦ on J♠ T♠ 5♥ — I have an open‑ended straight draw (K or 8 completes) and three queens to pair (≈11 outs to improve), plus positional advantage. I called his 3‑bet preflop to use position; this flop is a good spot to apply a small c‑bet to fold out weaker unpaired hands and low pocket pairs while retaining equity if called. I’ll bet small (~35% pot = 800) to seize initiative and keep the pot controllable. Plan: take the pot if he folds; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn — continue for value or fold to significant aggression if I miss.

I have Ace-King suited (diamonds) and I was the pre-flop 3-bettor. On a flop of Jack-Ten-Five with two spades, I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway straight. I checked to control the pot size out of position. Minni has bet 800 into a 2300 pot, which is roughly a 1/3 pot-sized bet. Given the pot odds (calling 800 to win a total pot of 3900, which is approximately 20.5%) and my equity (4 outs for the straight plus potentially 6 outs for the overcards), a call is the mathematically correct and standard play. Folding would be too tight given my equity, and raising all-in would likely only be called by hands that have me beaten or strong draws, whereas calling allows me to realize my equity and potentially see a turn card that improves my hand or provides more options.

On the turn, the five of diamonds pairs the board. I am out of position against an aggressive opponent who bet the flop. While I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw, I do not have a made hand yet. Checking is the most prudent move here to see how Minni reacts to the board pairing. This allows me to potentially see a free river card if she checks back, or evaluate the strength of her hand and my pot odds if she decides to bet again.

Turn pairs the board (5♣ pair) and Flash — who 3‑bet preflop and check‑called my flop c‑bet — is showing the kind of line that contains many medium/strong made hands and some spade two‑card draws. I have only drawing equity now (~11 outs to a pair/straight → ~11/46 ≈ 24% to improve to the river) and no made hand to extract reliable value. A turn c‑bet or shove as a bluff is doable against a very weak range, but Flash’s line and short effective stack make him unlikely to fold enough strong hands to make an aggressive line profitable long‑term. I’ll keep the pot small and realize my equity — check behind, see the river, and only invest more if I pair or complete the straight (or get a clear bluff‑catching spot on a favorable river).
