I don’t want to limp and invite a multiway pot against deep, aggressive stacks, so I’ll open to a standard 3x size to take the initiative and force folds or isolate one opponent. If I get a big 3-bet I’ll fold (hand is dominated); if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable runouts and proceed cautiously when I miss. Raising to 300 keeps things simple and maximizes my fold equity here.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six of spades. The Button (Minni) has raised to 3x the big blind. While Q6s has some potential as a suited hand, it is generally too weak to defend from the Small Blind against a 3x open, especially since I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Given that there are only a few hands remaining and I have a significant chip lead over my opponents, there is no need to take a marginal or negative expectation risk. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

9s4d is a very weak offsuit hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suited potential. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive in history, my equity is poor against their wide range. Pot odds are 200 to call into 450 (about 2.25:1, needing ~31% equity), but 94o realizes far less. BB defense should be tighter against late position aggression without playable features. Folding preserves my 7100 stack for better spots with 5 hands left.

I am on the button with Ace-Deuce offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. Given that I have a significant chip lead and there are only a few hands left in the game, I want to continue playing solid, aggressive poker to maintain my stack.

4s6h is a trash hand: offsuit, gapped, low cards, minimal straight or flush potential. OOP in SB facing aggressive button raise from Flash (who raises frequently per history). Pot odds ~2:1 but implied odds poor due to weak hand and position. Minni may defend BB, but no reason to speculate. Fold to preserve stack with only 5 hands left; discipline maximizes EV long-term.

Facing a 3x button open to 300 from Flash with the SB folded. Pot is 450 and I need 200 more to call, so I’m getting ~30.8% pot odds to see a flop. With ~103bb effective and a fairly wide button opening range, T♠8♣ has enough playability and equity heads-up to defend the big blind. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a marginal hand lacking strong blockers, so I’ll call and see a flop. Postflop plan: proceed cautiously — fold to heavy multi-street pressure on dry, high-card boards that miss me; call small c-bets with backdoor straights/flushes or decent equity and continue aggressively if I hit pair+ or strong draws; exploit any clear button-steal tendencies by floating or check-raising in the right spots.

Flop is 3c-9c-4d. I have T♠8♣ — no pair, one overcard to the 9 and only a runner-runner club flush draw (very low immediate equity). Flash opened wide preflop and checked the flop, showing weakness but also folding frequency that I could exploit with a small c-bet. With ~100bb effective and a marginal holding I prefer pot control and to realize equity rather than bloating a pot out of position. Plan: check back to keep the pot small and see a turn free. If Flash barrels a small c-bet I will often call to see cards (I can barrel back if I improve); I will fold to large sizing or a check-raise. If I pair the T or pick up a real draw on the turn I will take the initiative and bet/raise for value.

I am on the button with Ace-high on a dry board of 3c-9c-4d. My opponent, Minni, has checked to me. This board doesn't hit a wide Big Blind calling range particularly hard, although she could have middle pairs or small connectors. Betting here as a continuation bet is standard to pick up the pot immediately or to set up a fold later. With only five hands remaining in the match and a substantial lead, I will make a standard-sized continuation bet to maintain pressure.

Flop: 3c-9c-4d. I have T♠8♣ — missed but I do have one club (backdoor) and one overcard to the 9. Flash is a wide button opener and c-bets a small sizing (325), which prices me to call — I only need roughly ~25% equity to continue. Versus a wide continuation-range my hand has ~25–30% equity to improve (pairing a T or 8, plus rare runner-runner clubs, and the option to float and take the pot later). Stacks are deep (~100bb effective), so implied odds and post‑turn playability make a call reasonable while keeping the pot controlled. Plan: call the small c-bet, realize equity and look to fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve; if I pair or pick up a real draw I will take the initiative, and if Flash shows further weakness I can float and bluff later.

Board is 3♣-9♣-4♦-7♠, I hold T♠8♣. On the turn I picked up a real draw: an open‑ended straight draw (need a 6 or J — 8 outs) plus a nut-ish club flush draw (10 clubs remain). Union of straight+flush outs ≈ 16/46 ≈ 34.8% to improve by the river; there are also pair outs (3 tens + 3 eights) that can sometimes scoop small pots. Flash opened wide pre, c‑bet small on the flop and then checked the turn — a line consistent with a wide, miss-heavy range that will fold to pressure. I had already planned to take the initiative if I picked up a real draw on the turn, and I did. Betting here is a semi‑bluff: it gets folds from Ace/Kx and other air, builds the pot when I have ~35% to improve, and denies free cards that help some of his show-up hands. I don’t want to bloate the pot out of position, so I’ll size to roughly half‑pot — big enough to get folds and to extract value when I hit, but small enough to fold to significant aggression. Plan after this bet: if he folds we win the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (value‑bet if I make my straight/flush, check/call or bluff selectively if I miss); fold to a large check‑raise on the turn (I’m still a drawing hand).

Minni's lead on the turn after check-calling the flop suggests she has improved her hand or is holding a piece of the board that is currently beating my Ace-high. The 7s potentially completes a straight (56) or gives her two pair/a pair of sevens. Given that I have a massive stack lead and there are only five hands left in the match, there is no need to risk chips with a marginal Ace-high against showing aggression. I will fold and preserve my lead.

As5s is a strong suited ace hand on the button with excellent position against the blinds. It has high card strength, flush potential, straight potential (wheel), and great playability post-flop. With deep stacks and a small pot of 150, raising to 300 (3x the BB) is standard to steal blinds or build pot in position. Opponents are aggressive but folding equity is high pre-flop from blinds. No need to limp or call as raising applies pressure and defines ranges better.

Fold. 7♣8♥ offsuit is a marginal hand to defend from the small blind facing a button open to 300. I’ll be out of position post‑flop and likely to face action from the big blind as well. Calling 250 into a 450 pot commits me to a multiway, OOP pot with poor equity and weak playability; a min‑raise to 500 here would be a thin bluff that Elon will often continue against (or a 3‑bet could put me in a very uncomfortable spot). Effective stacks (~67BB vs Elon) mean I can wait for better, more playable hands (suited connectors, pairs, or hands with blockers). Preserve the stack for a higher EV spot — fold.

KTo is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind against a Button open. Elon's raise to 3x is standard, and with the pot odds being 2.25:1, I have the necessary equity to justify a call. I will be out of position post-flop, so I prefer calling over 3-betting to keep the pot manageable and see how the flop develops.

On this flop of Ac-Kd-Jh, I have middle pair (Kings) with a ten kicker and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). This board is quite favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range (Elon), as he can easily have Aces, better Kings, or strong broadway hands. Since I am out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand is strong enough to check-call most reasonably sized bets, but I shouldn't be leading into the pre-flop raiser on a texture that hits him so well.

Flop is Ac Kd Jh, rainbow board with broadway straight draws possible (QTo, T9s). I have As5s for top pair (aces) with king kicker, a very strong hand that blocks AA, AKs, AsX. Flash called pre-flop from BB and checked, representing possible weakness like mid pairs, weaker aces (AJo, ATo), king-high, or draws. Position on button allows control. Betting for value against worse (AJ, KQ, QJ, etc.) and protection vs. straight draws. No strong reason to check back with this equity. Pot 650, betting ~2/3 pot (450) builds pot while keeping fold equity vs. pure draws. Near end of session (hand 147/150), extract value to maximize stack.

I have middle pair (Kings) with a ten kicker and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts on a very heavy broadway board. While this board hits Elon's button-opening range extremely well, my hand has too much equity to fold to a single continuation bet. I am getting approximately 2.4:1 pot odds, which justifies a call to see if I can improve on the turn. I will check-call here and re-evaluate based on the turn card and Elon's subsequent action.

On the turn, the 7d is essentially a brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I still hold middle pair with a gutshot to the nut straight. Given the board is very heavy with broadway cards that hit the pre-flop raiser's range well, I will continue with the plan of checking to Elon. Leading here doesn't accomplish much, as I'm likely only called by better or forced to fold to a raise. By checking, I allow Elon to continue bluffing or check behind with weaker hands, and I can decide whether to call a bet based on the price offered.

Turn board brings 7d, adding a diamond flush draw but not completing obvious straights or improving many draws significantly. Board: Ac Kd Jh 7d. My As5s gives top pair (aces) with a weak 5 kicker, still ahead of most Kx, Jx, Qx, Tx combos, weaker Ax (AJo-ATo), and unfinished draws like QT or diamond draws. Flash's line: BB call preflop (wide range), check-call flop (shows strength but not monster), check turn (weakness or trapping). With position, pot 1550, and deep stacks (my 6300 vs Flash 21115), betting ~2/3 pot (1000) extracts value from worse hands while protecting against draws/river cards. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or missing value. Near session end (147/150), apply pressure to build/maximize stack without overplaying.

On the turn, the 7d is a blank card. I currently hold middle pair (Kings) with a ten kicker and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (any Queen). Elon, the pre-flop aggressor on the button, has bet 1000 into a pot of 1550, which is a strong 2/3 pot-sized bet. This board is extremely favorable for Elon's range, as it contains an Ace and a King, as well as many broadway combinations. However, I am getting roughly 2.5:1 pot odds to call (requiring about 28% equity). While I am likely behind an Ace or two-pair, my middle pair and nut straight draw provide enough equity to justify one more call, especially considering the potential implied odds if a Queen hits the river and I can extract more from Elon's remaining stack. I will call and re-evaluate on the river.

On the river, the board is Ac-Kd-Jh-7d-5d. I hold middle pair (Kings) with a Ten kicker. This board is very dangerous as many of Elon's opening hands (Aces, better Kings, and the straight QT) are ahead of me. Since I am out of position and have been check-calling the previous streets, the most standard and prudent play is to check to the aggressor. Leading here wouldn't provide much value, as I'm mostly likely to be called by hands that have me beat. By checking, I can potentially see a cheap showdown if Elon checks back or decide whether to call a final bet based on the size and my read of his aggression.

River 5d pairs my 5s, giving me two pair: aces and fives (As/Ac/5s/5d) with top kicker effectively. Strong hand on Ac Kd Jh 7d 5d board, beating all one-pair hands (AJ, AQo, KQs, JTs etc.), most two-pairs (like KQ, KJ), and unfinished draws. Flash's passive check-call lines preflop/flop/turn/river suggest medium strength like top pair good kicker, mid two-pair, or a flush draw that may have hit but is trapping or pot-controlling. Sets (KK/JJ/77) possible but unlikely given wide BB call pre and no prior aggression. Diamond flush possible but his river check reduces likelihood of massive value trap. With position, pot 3550, my stack 5300 (effective), and session near end (147/150), value bet ~2/3 pot to extract from worse hands (AJ, KQ, etc.) that call while denying equity to bluffs/draws. Sizing 2300 builds pot, offers good SPR, and maximizes EV.

On a board of Ac-Kd-Jh-7d-5d, my hand (Kh-Ts) is middle pair with a Ten kicker. This board is extremely favorable for Elon, who raised pre-flop from the button. His range includes many hands that have me soundly beaten, such as any Ace (AA, AK, AQ, AJ, AT, etc.), better Kings (KQ), two-pair (KJ), or the straight (QT). Elon has bet on every street, and his river bet of 2300 into a pot of 3550 represents significant strength. While I am getting decent pot odds (approximately 2.5:1), my hand is essentially a weak bluff-catcher on a board that hits the aggressor's range perfectly. Given the high probability that Elon holds at least an Ace or better, a fold is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I’m on the button with 5♥3♥ — a weak but suited hand with good playability in position. Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to see a flop; folding is too tight and limping hands from the button invites the blinds to take initiative. Standard exploit here is to open a wide stealing range and take the initiative: raise to the 2xBB (200) size. That size preserves pot control, gives solid fold equity vs both blinds, and keeps post‑flop decisions manageable. Plan: fold to a sizable 3‑bet, otherwise use positional advantage — c‑bet on favorable, dry flops and play cautiously on coordinated or heavily‑drawn boards.

With only three hands remaining in the session and a substantial chip lead, my primary objective is to minimize risk and protect my stack. King-Four offsuit is a weak hand to play from the Small Blind out of position against a Button raiser. Calling would put me in a difficult post-flop situation where I am likely to lose more chips if I don't hit the board strongly. Given the pot odds are okay but not incredible, folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my lead.

J4o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low card strength. Facing a button min-raise to 200 (2x), my BB defense range doesn't include this trash hand. Equity is insufficient against Minni's likely wide but decent button opening range (which includes many better hands). Postflop playability is awful—no suitedness, no straight potential. Deep stacks mean better spots await. Standard fold to preserve stack near end of session.

With only two hands remaining in the game (Hand 149 and 150) and a significant chip lead, my primary objective is to protect my stack and minimize risk. 8s 6h is a very weak hand. While I am on the button and could attempt to steal the blinds, the potential for a 3-bet from the blinds or a difficult post-flop scenario is not worth the risk relative to the small reward. I will fold to preserve my lead.

7s2d is one of the worst possible starting hands in Hold'em, with extremely low equity against any reasonable big blind defending range. Pot odds to complete the small blind are about 3:1 (50 to call into 150), but even against the widest possible range, my hand has minimal playability, no straight or flush potential worth noting, and poor post-flop prospects out of position. No reads or history suggest Minni is folding her blind often enough to justify a raise. Standard play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots near the end of the 150-hand session.

3-handed on the button with deep stacks in the final hand. 4h5d is a playable hand from this position due to its straight potential and post-flop playability in position. Standard opening range here includes low offsuit connectors like this for implied odds against deep stacks. Opponents have shown aggression but also defend wide in blinds. Raising builds the pot and takes initiative. Limp invites multiway pots OOP for them; fold is too tight. Raise to 3x BB standard size.

Facing a BTN open to 300 from three-handed with Qc3h in the small blind. This hand is a weak, dominated queen with poor postflop playability out of position and vulnerable to multiway action if the big blind defends. A light 3-bet is unattractive (few blockers, bad stack dynamics) and calling to play OOP is a marginal, exploitable spot. Preserve bankroll and wait for a better spot — fold and surrender the small blind.

On the final hand of the session, I am holding Ten-Eight of spades in the Big Blind. Elon has opened from the Button for 300. Given the pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 450) and the playability of suited connectors/gappers, this is a standard defend. I will call to see a flop and look for an opportunity to maximize my stack one last time, while remaining mindful of Elon's aggressive post-flop tendencies.

On the final hand of the game, I find myself on a very high-card board (Ace-King-Jack) after defending my big blind with Ten-Eight of spades. This board texture heavily favors Elon's range as the button raiser, as it hits almost all of his broadway cards and high pairs. While I do have a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway straight (needing a Queen), I am out of position and have no made hand. The most standard and prudent play here is to check to the aggressor and see how he proceeds. I will likely fold to a significant bet given the strength of his range on this specific board, but checking allows me to potentially see a free turn card if he decides to check back.

Final hand of 150, heads-up vs Flash in BB after Minni folded SB. Flop Jc Kh Ad hits my pre-flop raising range (broadway, Ax) hard. My 4h5d is pure air: no pair, weak gutshot straight draw (to 36 straight, unlikely), no flush draw despite Kh. Pot 650, Flash checks first (weakness from wide defending range). Betting as bluff has some merit (button image, rep top pair/top kicker like AK/AJ), but board is drawish enough (str8 draws like QT, flush draws if suited) that Flash can continue with medium strength or float. Risk of check-raise high. Better to check behind, control pot with zero showdown value, realize tiny equity for free, and re-evaluate turn. Deep stacks, no need to force action pre-turn with nothing. Goal: maximize stack long-term, not gamble last hand.

On the turn, the Six of Hearts is a complete blank. On the flop, both Elon and I checked on a board (A-K-J) that heavily favors the Button's opening range. Elon's check-back on the flop suggests he either has a marginal made hand (like a weak King or a Jack) that he wants to take to showdown cheaply, or he has completely missed. With my Ten-Eight of spades, I still only have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). Since I am out of position and have no reason to believe Elon will fold a better hand on this board, checking again is the most prudent play. It allows me to potentially see a free river card and realize my equity if Elon checks back again.

Final hand #150, heads-up vs Flash on turn with pot 650 and 8400 effective stack. Board Jc Kh Ad 6h is highly coordinated with broadway potential, heart flush draws (Kh6h), and straight draws (QT, 23 wheel, etc.). My 4h5d is complete air: no pair, no strong draws (backdoor straight filler at best with 3x or 7x, one heart for weak flush backdoor). Preflop raise checked through flop reps possible weakness or trap, but Flash's double check suggests medium strength, draw, or weak hand unwilling to bet OOP. Bluffing turn risks check-raise from draws/pairs or call from top pair; no fold equity against BB defender who calls pre wide. Checking realizes ~10-15% equity to river for free, controls pot size, avoids spew in last hand where ICM irrelevant but stack maximization prioritizes not gambling air. Deep stacks but small pot not worth risking. Check back standard.

On the river, the board is Jc-Kh-Ad-6h-7h. I have Ten-high and no showdown value. Elon has checked back both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a weak-to-marginal hand. Since it is the final hand of the session and the pot is relatively small, I will check and see if I can get a free showdown or simply fold if Elon bets, prioritizing the preservation of my stack.

Last hand: steal pot or showdown lose. Bet standard.

On the final hand of the game, the board has run out Jack-King-Ace-Six-Seven with no possible flush (except the hearts which I don't have). I hold Ten-high, which has virtually zero showdown value. Elon checked back the flop and turn, which normally suggests a weak hand, but even his weakest 'showdown' hands like small pocket pairs (22-55) or a random Seven or Six would beat my Ten-high. He has now bet 325 into 650 on the river. While it is possible he is bluffing, I cannot win this pot unless he is bluffing with exactly Nine-high or lower. Given the pot odds and the low probability of Ten-high being the best hand, the most disciplined play is to fold and conclude the session.
